description: Gospel principles from For the Strength of Youth — scenarios about making choices that follow Jesus Christ.

1.definition: Jesus Christ loves you and wants to help you.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You made a mistake and broke your mom's favorite cup. You feel really bad about it. What should you remember?
1.scenarioYouDo: You made a mistake and broke your mom's favorite cup. You feel really bad about it. What should you remember?

challenge.scenario: You made a mistake and broke your mom's favorite cup. You feel really bad about it. What should you remember?
options: You should hide what you did so nobody finds out
You're a bad person for breaking it
Jesus loves you even when you make mistakes

1.tip: Jesus loves you no matter what — even when you mess up! (John 3:16)

2.definition: Jesus Christ's love for you doesn't depend on you being perfect.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You said something hurtful to a friend and now you feel guilty. A classmate says, 'Don't worry about it — everyone says stuff like that.' What's the best response?
2.scenarioYouDo: You said something hurtful to a friend and now you feel guilty. A classmate says, 'Don't worry about it — everyone says stuff like that.' What's the best response?

challenge.scenario: You said something hurtful to a friend and now you feel guilty. A classmate says, 'Don't worry about it — everyone says stuff like that.' What's the best response?
options: Recognize the guilt as a nudge to apologize and do better
Ignore the guilt because your classmate is right
Decide you're a terrible person and avoid your friend forever
Pretend it never happened and move on

2.tip: Feeling guilty can be a gift — it shows you care and points you toward making things right. (Romans 5:8)

3.definition: The Savior's love is constant and personal — He knows your struggles and reaches out to you individually.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You've been going through a hard time and feel distant from God. A friend says, 'God only helps people who have it all together.' How do you respond?
3.scenarioYouDo: You've been going through a hard time and feel distant from God. A friend says, 'God only helps people who have it all together.' How do you respond?

challenge.scenario: You've been going through a hard time and feel distant from God. A friend says, 'God only helps people who have it all together.' How do you respond?
options: Agree — you need to fix everything before God will care
Accept that maybe God has given up on you
Decide religion isn't for people with real problems
Share that Jesus Christ reaches out to us especially when we're struggling

3.tip: Christ descended below all things so He could reach you wherever you are. (Alma 7:11-12)

1.definition: Repentance isn't a punishment — it's a gift that helps us feel better.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You took a toy from your sibling without asking and now feel bad inside. What is repentance?
1.scenarioYouDo: You took a toy from your sibling without asking and now feel bad inside. What is repentance?

challenge.scenario: You took a toy from your sibling without asking and now feel bad inside. What is repentance?
options: Getting in trouble for what you did
A way to say sorry, fix things, and feel happy again
Something only really bad people have to do

1.tip: Repentance is like a reset button that helps you feel the Savior's love again. (Mosiah 26:30)

2.definition: Repentance is not punishment for sin — it's the way the Savior frees us from sin.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend at church says, 'I hate repenting — it's like being grounded by God.' What would you say?
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend at church says, 'I hate repenting — it's like being grounded by God.' What would you say?

challenge.scenario: A friend at church says, 'I hate repenting — it's like being grounded by God.' What would you say?
options: You're right, repentance is basically God's way of punishing us
Just don't sin and you won't have to worry about it
Repentance is only for serious sins, not everyday stuff
Actually, repentance is how Jesus frees us — it's a gift, not a punishment

2.tip: Repentance isn't a penalty — it's the path the Savior made so you can be free. (2 Nephi 2:27)

3.definition: Repentance is liberating, not shaming. The Savior designed it to free you, not condemn you.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A friend who recently returned to church activity says they feel too ashamed to repent of past choices. They think God is disappointed in them. What do you share?
3.scenarioYouDo: A friend who recently returned to church activity says they feel too ashamed to repent of past choices. They think God is disappointed in them. What do you share?

challenge.scenario: A friend who recently returned to church activity says they feel too ashamed to repent of past choices. They think God is disappointed in them. What do you share?
options: Tell them some mistakes really are too big to come back from
Suggest they just stop thinking about the past and move forward without addressing it
Explain that repentance is the Savior's way of removing shame, not adding to it
Recommend they wait until they feel worthy enough to approach God

3.tip: The Savior doesn't wait for you to be clean before He helps — He's the one who makes you clean. (Isaiah 1:18)

1.definition: To repent means to turn away from bad choices and turn toward God.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You've been saying unkind things about a kid at school. You start to feel bad about it. What does it mean to repent?
1.scenarioYouDo: You've been saying unkind things about a kid at school. You start to feel bad about it. What does it mean to repent?

challenge.scenario: You've been saying unkind things about a kid at school. You start to feel bad about it. What does it mean to repent?
options: Stop saying unkind things and start being kind instead
Just feel bad about it and hope it goes away
Say sorry but keep doing the same thing

1.tip: Repentance means doing a U-turn — away from wrong and toward what's right! (Mosiah 5:2)

2.definition: Repentance is more than feeling sorry — it's a real change in direction, turning away from sin and toward God.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You've been copying homework answers from a friend. You feel guilty but the easy grades are tempting. What does true repentance look like?
2.scenarioYouDo: You've been copying homework answers from a friend. You feel guilty but the easy grades are tempting. What does true repentance look like?

challenge.scenario: You've been copying homework answers from a friend. You feel guilty but the easy grades are tempting. What does true repentance look like?
options: Feel bad but keep copying since the grades matter more
Stop copying, talk to your teacher if needed, and commit to doing your own work
Stop only because you're afraid of getting caught
Promise to stop but figure you'll start again next semester

2.tip: Real repentance changes what you do, not just how you feel. (Ezekiel 18:21-22)

3.definition: To repent is to undergo a fundamental change of heart — reorienting your desires and actions toward God.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You realize you've developed a habit of being dishonest in small ways — exaggerating stories, bending the truth to look good. It doesn't feel like a 'big sin' but it's affecting who you're becoming. What do you do?
3.scenarioYouDo: You realize you've developed a habit of being dishonest in small ways — exaggerating stories, bending the truth to look good. It doesn't feel like a 'big sin' but it's affecting who you're becoming. What do you do?

challenge.scenario: You realize you've developed a habit of being dishonest in small ways — exaggerating stories, bending the truth to look good. It doesn't feel like a 'big sin' but it's affecting who you're becoming. What do you do?
options: Recognize this as something to repent of now, and intentionally practice honesty even when it's uncomfortable
Everyone exaggerates — it's not really dishonesty
Wait until it becomes a bigger problem before worrying about it
Confess to everyone you've ever exaggerated to

3.tip: Repentance isn't just for big mistakes — it's the daily practice of becoming who God knows you can be. (Alma 5:26)

1.definition: Jesus suffered for your sins so you don't have to carry that weight alone.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You feel really sad because you lied to your teacher. It feels heavy inside. Who already made it possible for you to feel better?
1.scenarioYouDo: You feel really sad because you lied to your teacher. It feels heavy inside. Who already made it possible for you to feel better?

challenge.scenario: You feel really sad because you lied to your teacher. It feels heavy inside. Who already made it possible for you to feel better?
options: Your best friend
Nobody — you have to fix it all by yourself
Jesus Christ, because He already paid the price for your mistakes

1.tip: Jesus already did the hard part — all you have to do is come to Him. (Alma 7:13)

2.definition: Jesus Christ took upon Himself your sins so you can repent and keep progressing.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You've been struggling with a bad habit and feel like you keep failing. You start to wonder if there's any point in trying anymore. What's true?
2.scenarioYouDo: You've been struggling with a bad habit and feel like you keep failing. You start to wonder if there's any point in trying anymore. What's true?

challenge.scenario: You've been struggling with a bad habit and feel like you keep failing. You start to wonder if there's any point in trying anymore. What's true?
options: If you keep struggling, it means you're not really repenting
Some habits are just too strong for repentance to work
You should feel ashamed every time you slip up
Because of Jesus Christ's Atonement, you can keep trying and progressing even when it's hard

2.tip: The Atonement isn't just for one-time fixes — it powers your ongoing progress. (Moroni 10:32-33)

3.definition: Christ's Atonement is infinite — it covers not only your sins but your pain, weakness, and struggle to change.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A friend confides that they've been trying to overcome a personal struggle for months. They say, 'I've repented so many times — I don't think the Atonement works for me.' What do you share?
3.scenarioYouDo: A friend confides that they've been trying to overcome a personal struggle for months. They say, 'I've repented so many times — I don't think the Atonement works for me.' What do you share?

challenge.scenario: A friend confides that they've been trying to overcome a personal struggle for months. They say, 'I've repented so many times — I don't think the Atonement works for me.' What do you share?
options: Maybe their problem is beyond what the Atonement covers
Share that the Atonement covers the whole journey — including the struggle — and that repeated effort is part of repentance, not a sign of failure
Suggest they just try harder and use more willpower
Tell them to stop worrying about it — nobody's perfect

3.tip: Struggling doesn't mean failing. Christ's grace meets you in the effort, not just at the finish line. (Ether 12:27)

1.definition: When you repent, Jesus Christ forgives you and helps you feel whole again.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You were mean to your little brother and said sorry. You've tried hard to be nicer. But you still feel bad. What should you know?
1.scenarioYouDo: You were mean to your little brother and said sorry. You've tried hard to be nicer. But you still feel bad. What should you know?

challenge.scenario: You were mean to your little brother and said sorry. You've tried hard to be nicer. But you still feel bad. What should you know?
options: You'll always feel bad for what you did
Saying sorry doesn't really count
When you repent, Jesus forgives you and helps the bad feelings go away

1.tip: When Jesus forgives you, He doesn't hold it against you — ever! (D&C 58:42)

2.definition: Jesus Christ doesn't just forgive — He heals. Repentance makes you whole, not just pardoned.
2.scenarioDoneTo: After making a hurtful choice you've fully repented of, you still carry guilt and shame. A leader says, 'You've already been forgiven.' Why is this hard to believe?
2.scenarioYouDo: After making a hurtful choice you've fully repented of, you still carry guilt and shame. A leader says, 'You've already been forgiven.' Why is this hard to believe?

challenge.scenario: After making a hurtful choice you've fully repented of, you still carry guilt and shame. A leader says, 'You've already been forgiven.' Why is this hard to believe?
options: Because forgiveness means you're only pardoned, not actually changed
Because you probably haven't repented enough yet
Because real forgiveness doesn't happen until the next life
Because sometimes we hang onto guilt even after the Savior has already let it go — trusting in His healing is part of repentance

2.tip: Letting go of guilt after sincere repentance is an act of faith in the Savior's power to heal. (3 Nephi 9:13)

3.definition: Christ's forgiveness doesn't merely erase the record — it transforms you, healing the wounds that sin and sorrow leave behind.
3.scenarioDoneTo: Someone you're mentoring says, 'I know God forgives, but I can't forgive myself for what I did years ago.' They've sincerely repented. How do you help?
3.scenarioYouDo: Someone you're mentoring says, 'I know God forgives, but I can't forgive myself for what I did years ago.' They've sincerely repented. How do you help?

challenge.scenario: Someone you're mentoring says, 'I know God forgives, but I can't forgive myself for what I did years ago.' They've sincerely repented. How do you help?
options: Tell them self-forgiveness will come naturally with time — just wait it out
Agree — some things are just too hard to get over
Help them see that refusing to forgive themselves after Christ has forgiven them may actually be a form of not trusting His Atonement fully
Suggest they do extra service to earn the right to feel forgiven

3.tip: When you hold onto guilt Christ has already removed, you're carrying a burden He already set down. (Alma 36:19-21)

1.definition: Repentance is something we do every day, not just once.
1.scenarioDoneTo: After Family Home Evening, your dad says everyone should try to repent a little every day. Your younger sibling asks, 'But I haven't done anything really bad!' What do you say?
1.scenarioYouDo: After Family Home Evening, your dad says everyone should try to repent a little every day. Your younger sibling asks, 'But I haven't done anything really bad!' What do you say?

challenge.scenario: After Family Home Evening, your dad says everyone should try to repent a little every day. Your younger sibling asks, 'But I haven't done anything really bad!' What do you say?
options: That's right — repentance is only for big mistakes
Repentance means trying to be a little better every day, even with small things
You only need to repent when the bishop tells you to

1.tip: Repentance isn't only for big problems — it's your daily path to becoming more like Jesus. (Alma 34:33)

2.definition: Repentance is an ongoing process — it's not something you do once and then you're done.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend says, 'I already repented of that — why do I need to keep working on it?' What would you say?
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend says, 'I already repented of that — why do I need to keep working on it?' What would you say?

challenge.scenario: A friend says, 'I already repented of that — why do I need to keep working on it?' What would you say?
options: They're right — once you repent, you're done
They should only worry if they commit the same sin again
Repentance is a lifelong journey of turning to Christ, not a one-time checklist
Once you've talked to the bishop, the process is officially finished

2.tip: Repentance isn't a destination — it's the road you walk every single day with the Savior. (2 Nephi 31:20)

3.definition: Repentance is not a one-time event but a continual process of aligning your life with Christ.
3.scenarioDoneTo: In a gospel discussion, someone argues, 'If you truly repented, you'd never struggle with the same weakness again.' How do you respond?
3.scenarioYouDo: In a gospel discussion, someone argues, 'If you truly repented, you'd never struggle with the same weakness again.' How do you respond?

challenge.scenario: In a gospel discussion, someone argues, 'If you truly repented, you'd never struggle with the same weakness again.' How do you respond?
options: Agree — real repentance means the problem goes away completely
Explain that ongoing repentance acknowledges our mortal nature and our continual need for Christ's grace
Say that struggling means they probably weren't sincere the first time
Suggest they aren't praying hard enough

3.tip: Daily repentance isn't a sign of failure — it's the mark of someone who keeps choosing Christ. (D&C 64:10)

1.definition: Jesus Christ invites everyone to come unto Him.
1.scenarioDoneTo: A new kid at church looks nervous and says, 'I don't know if I belong here — I don't know anything about Jesus.' What do you say?
1.scenarioYouDo: A new kid at church looks nervous and says, 'I don't know if I belong here — I don't know anything about Jesus.' What do you say?

challenge.scenario: A new kid at church looks nervous and says, 'I don't know if I belong here — I don't know anything about Jesus.' What do you say?
options: Jesus wants everyone to come to Him — you don't have to be perfect or know everything
You have to know a lot about Jesus before you can come to church
You should probably go to a different church that's easier

1.tip: Jesus's invitation is for everyone — no experience needed! (3 Nephi 9:14)

2.definition: The Savior's invitation to 'come unto me' is open to everyone — regardless of where they've been.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A classmate who hasn't been to church in a while says, 'I've done too many bad things — Jesus probably doesn't want me back.' What do you say?
2.scenarioYouDo: A classmate who hasn't been to church in a while says, 'I've done too many bad things — Jesus probably doesn't want me back.' What do you say?

challenge.scenario: A classmate who hasn't been to church in a while says, 'I've done too many bad things — Jesus probably doesn't want me back.' What do you say?
options: You're right — you should get your life together first before going back
Maybe just wait until you feel ready
Jesus is always ready to welcome you back — that's the whole point of His Atonement
Only go back if you're sure you won't mess up again

2.tip: You don't have to be perfect to come to Christ — you just have to come. (Matthew 11:28-30)

3.definition: Coming unto Christ is not about arriving at a state of perfection — it's about continually moving toward Him.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A young adult in your ward says they want to 'get their life together' before they start fully participating in the gospel again. They feel they need to be worthy first. What counsel might you offer?
3.scenarioYouDo: A young adult in your ward says they want to 'get their life together' before they start fully participating in the gospel again. They feel they need to be worthy first. What counsel might you offer?

challenge.scenario: A young adult in your ward says they want to 'get their life together' before they start fully participating in the gospel again. They feel they need to be worthy first. What counsel might you offer?
options: Gently share that coming to Christ is what makes us whole — we don't have to be whole before we come
Support their plan — it's important to be worthy before approaching God
Tell them the Church doesn't need people who aren't fully committed
Suggest they set a date in the future to start coming back

3.tip: Christ doesn't ask you to clean up before you come — He asks you to come so He can help you. (2 Nephi 26:33)

1.definition: You can talk to Heavenly Father anytime through prayer.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You're feeling scared at bedtime because of a thunderstorm. What can you do?
1.scenarioYouDo: You're feeling scared at bedtime because of a thunderstorm. What can you do?

challenge.scenario: You're feeling scared at bedtime because of a thunderstorm. What can you do?
options: Just hide under the blankets and hope it stops
Wait until Sunday to talk to God about it
Pray and ask Heavenly Father to help you feel calm and safe

1.tip: Heavenly Father is always listening — you can talk to Him anytime, anywhere! (3 Nephi 18:20)

2.definition: Prayer is how you communicate with Heavenly Father — He wants to hear from you about everything in your life.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You have a big decision to make about whether to try out for a school team. You're not sure if that's something worth praying about. What do you think?
2.scenarioYouDo: You have a big decision to make about whether to try out for a school team. You're not sure if that's something worth praying about. What do you think?

challenge.scenario: You have a big decision to make about whether to try out for a school team. You're not sure if that's something worth praying about. What do you think?
options: Prayer is only for spiritual things, not everyday decisions
You should only pray when you're in serious trouble
Heavenly Father cares about all parts of your life — it's always worth praying about
Just make the decision yourself — God is too busy for small stuff

2.tip: No concern is too small for Heavenly Father — He cares about every part of your life. (Alma 37:37)

3.definition: Meaningful prayer requires sincerity and effort — it's a real conversation with your Heavenly Father, not just a ritual.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You realize your prayers have become repetitive and feel more like a routine than a conversation. You still pray daily but it feels hollow. What should you do?
3.scenarioYouDo: You realize your prayers have become repetitive and feel more like a routine than a conversation. You still pray daily but it feels hollow. What should you do?

challenge.scenario: You realize your prayers have become repetitive and feel more like a routine than a conversation. You still pray daily but it feels hollow. What should you do?
options: Stop praying until you feel more spiritual
Keep going through the motions — consistency is all that matters
Switch to only praying when you really need something
Try to deepen your prayers by speaking honestly, listening, and studying before you pray

3.tip: If your prayers feel empty, don't stop praying — pray differently. (D&C 9:7-8)

1.definition: Reading the scriptures helps you learn what Heavenly Father wants you to know.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your family is reading the Book of Mormon together, but you'd rather play a game. Why is scripture study good for you?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your family is reading the Book of Mormon together, but you'd rather play a game. Why is scripture study good for you?

challenge.scenario: Your family is reading the Book of Mormon together, but you'd rather play a game. Why is scripture study good for you?
options: The scriptures teach you about Jesus and help you make good choices
It's boring and doesn't help with anything
You only need to read them when you're old enough

1.tip: The scriptures are like a letter from Heavenly Father — full of love and direction for you! (2 Timothy 3:16)

2.definition: Studying the scriptures and the words of living prophets helps you recognize the voice of the Spirit.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend says, 'I don't need to read the scriptures — I can just feel what's right.' How do you respond?
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend says, 'I don't need to read the scriptures — I can just feel what's right.' How do you respond?

challenge.scenario: A friend says, 'I don't need to read the scriptures — I can just feel what's right.' How do you respond?
options: They're right — feelings are more reliable than scriptures
Scriptures are only useful for seminary class
Only read scriptures when you have a specific problem
Scripture study trains your mind and heart to recognize truth and the Spirit's voice

2.tip: The scriptures tune your heart to recognize the Spirit — like learning a language you already feel. (2 Nephi 32:3)

3.definition: Consistent scripture study and attention to prophetic counsel provide a foundation for receiving personal revelation.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're facing a major life decision and praying for guidance, but you haven't been studying the scriptures regularly. You feel like you're not getting clear answers. What might help?
3.scenarioYouDo: You're facing a major life decision and praying for guidance, but you haven't been studying the scriptures regularly. You feel like you're not getting clear answers. What might help?

challenge.scenario: You're facing a major life decision and praying for guidance, but you haven't been studying the scriptures regularly. You feel like you're not getting clear answers. What might help?
options: Commit to consistent scripture study and prophetic counsel as a foundation for the revelation you're seeking
Pray louder and more urgently
Accept that God just doesn't want to answer right now
Ask someone else to get the answer for you

3.tip: Revelation often flows through prepared channels — scripture study keeps those channels open. (D&C 18:36)

1.definition: The Holy Ghost helps you feel peaceful and know what's right.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You're trying to decide whether to play with a group of kids who are being mean to someone. You feel uneasy inside. What's happening?
1.scenarioYouDo: You're trying to decide whether to play with a group of kids who are being mean to someone. You feel uneasy inside. What's happening?

challenge.scenario: You're trying to decide whether to play with a group of kids who are being mean to someone. You feel uneasy inside. What's happening?
options: You're just being a scaredy-cat
Nothing — feelings don't mean anything
The Holy Ghost is helping you feel that something isn't right

1.tip: That quiet feeling inside is often the Holy Ghost helping you choose the right! (D&C 8:2)

2.definition: The Spirit speaks through feelings of peace, comfort, guidance, and sometimes warning.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You're invited to a party but feel uneasy about going even though your friends insist it'll be fine. What do you do?
2.scenarioYouDo: You're invited to a party but feel uneasy about going even though your friends insist it'll be fine. What do you do?

challenge.scenario: You're invited to a party but feel uneasy about going even though your friends insist it'll be fine. What do you do?
options: Pay attention to the uneasy feeling because it might be the Spirit warning you
Ignore the feeling — your friends know best
Only trust feelings that tell you to do fun things
Go anyway and see what happens — you can always leave

2.tip: The Spirit's warnings feel different from fear — they come with a calm clarity about what you should do. (Galatians 5:22-23)

3.definition: The Holy Ghost communicates in diverse ways — peace, clarity, ideas, feelings, and sometimes restraint — and learning to discern His voice takes practice.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're praying about a big decision and expect a dramatic answer, but instead you just feel a quiet calm when you think about one path. A friend says that's 'just your own feelings.' How do you respond?
3.scenarioYouDo: You're praying about a big decision and expect a dramatic answer, but instead you just feel a quiet calm when you think about one path. A friend says that's 'just your own feelings.' How do you respond?

challenge.scenario: You're praying about a big decision and expect a dramatic answer, but instead you just feel a quiet calm when you think about one path. A friend says that's 'just your own feelings.' How do you respond?
options: Agree — it's probably just your preference, not the Spirit
Explain that the Spirit often communicates through quiet peace and clarity — and that learning to trust those promptings is part of spiritual growth
Demand a more dramatic answer from God before deciding
Flip a coin since prayer didn't give a clear answer

3.tip: The Spirit usually whispers — learning to hear Him in the quiet is one of life's most important skills. (1 Kings 19:11-12)

1.definition: When you make good choices, it's easier to feel the Holy Ghost.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You chose to be kind to a kid who was sitting alone at lunch. Now you feel warm and happy inside. Why?
1.scenarioYouDo: You chose to be kind to a kid who was sitting alone at lunch. Now you feel warm and happy inside. Why?

challenge.scenario: You chose to be kind to a kid who was sitting alone at lunch. Now you feel warm and happy inside. Why?
options: You probably just ate something good for lunch
Making a good choice helped you feel the Holy Ghost
It's just a coincidence

1.tip: Good choices invite good feelings — that's the Holy Ghost cheering you on! (Mosiah 2:41)

2.definition: Your daily choices directly affect your ability to feel and follow the Spirit.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You notice that when you spend a lot of time on social media and skip personal prayer, you feel more confused and less peaceful. What's the connection?
2.scenarioYouDo: You notice that when you spend a lot of time on social media and skip personal prayer, you feel more confused and less peaceful. What's the connection?

challenge.scenario: You notice that when you spend a lot of time on social media and skip personal prayer, you feel more confused and less peaceful. What's the connection?
options: There's no connection — prayer and social media are unrelated
Your daily habits and choices affect how clearly you can feel the Spirit
You just need to find better accounts to follow
Feeling confused is normal for teenagers — it has nothing to do with the Spirit

2.tip: The Spirit doesn't leave — but our choices can make His voice harder to hear. (Helaman 4:24)

3.definition: Every choice either strengthens or weakens your sensitivity to the Spirit. Spiritual receptivity is cultivated through consistent righteous living.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A friend says, 'I don't feel the Spirit like I used to.' As you talk, you learn they've gradually let go of several personal standards. They blame God for the distance. What insight can you offer?
3.scenarioYouDo: A friend says, 'I don't feel the Spirit like I used to.' As you talk, you learn they've gradually let go of several personal standards. They blame God for the distance. What insight can you offer?

challenge.scenario: A friend says, 'I don't feel the Spirit like I used to.' As you talk, you learn they've gradually let go of several personal standards. They blame God for the distance. What insight can you offer?
options: God does sometimes withdraw for no reason — there's nothing they can do
Suggest they just try to feel more spiritual during church
Tell them to stop overthinking it
Help them see that small, gradual choices can create distance from the Spirit, and that returning to those standards can restore that connection

3.tip: The Spirit is always broadcasting — our choices determine how clearly we receive the signal. (D&C 121:37)

1.definition: Heavenly Father speaks to us through living prophets.
1.scenarioDoneTo: In general conference, the prophet asks everyone to be kinder to their families. Should you listen?
1.scenarioYouDo: In general conference, the prophet asks everyone to be kinder to their families. Should you listen?

challenge.scenario: In general conference, the prophet asks everyone to be kinder to their families. Should you listen?
options: No — that's just an old man's opinion
Only if your parents agree with what he said
Yes — the prophet speaks for Heavenly Father and gives us direction

1.tip: Prophets are like Heavenly Father's messengers — they bring His words right to you! (Amos 3:7)

2.definition: Living prophets receive revelation for our time, and following their counsel protects and blesses you.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend says, 'The prophet's counsel is outdated — the world has moved on.' How do you respond?
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend says, 'The prophet's counsel is outdated — the world has moved on.' How do you respond?

challenge.scenario: A friend says, 'The prophet's counsel is outdated — the world has moved on.' How do you respond?
options: They're right — prophets can't keep up with modern life
Prophets receive revelation for our specific time — their counsel is current and inspired
Only follow the prophet when it's convenient
The prophet's words are suggestions, not real guidance

2.tip: Prophets aren't behind the times — they're ahead of them. (D&C 1:38)

3.definition: Following the counsel of living prophets requires faith, especially when their guidance goes against popular culture.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A prophetic teaching feels countercultural and you face pressure from peers to dismiss it. You personally haven't received a strong witness about it yet. What do you do?
3.scenarioYouDo: A prophetic teaching feels countercultural and you face pressure from peers to dismiss it. You personally haven't received a strong witness about it yet. What do you do?

challenge.scenario: A prophetic teaching feels countercultural and you face pressure from peers to dismiss it. You personally haven't received a strong witness about it yet. What do you do?
options: Reject the teaching since you haven't had a personal confirmation
Follow the crowd — cultural consensus is more reliable than prophets
Exercise faith by following prophetic counsel while sincerely seeking your own confirmation through study and prayer
Wait until the Church changes its stance

3.tip: Sometimes faith means obeying while you're still learning — trust can precede understanding. (Proverbs 3:5-6)

1.definition: Heavenly Father will help you know what to do if you ask Him.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You have two friends who both want you to play with them at recess and you don't know what to do. What can you try?
1.scenarioYouDo: You have two friends who both want you to play with them at recess and you don't know what to do. What can you try?

challenge.scenario: You have two friends who both want you to play with them at recess and you don't know what to do. What can you try?
options: Just pick whoever is most popular
Ask Heavenly Father to help you know the right thing to do
Don't play with either of them to avoid the problem

1.tip: When you don't know what to do, ask Heavenly Father — He loves to help! (James 1:5)

2.definition: You can receive personal revelation to guide your own life — God wants to direct you personally.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You need to choose between two good opportunities — an advanced class and a service project that meet at the same time. Both seem great. How do you decide?
2.scenarioYouDo: You need to choose between two good opportunities — an advanced class and a service project that meet at the same time. Both seem great. How do you decide?

challenge.scenario: You need to choose between two good opportunities — an advanced class and a service project that meet at the same time. Both seem great. How do you decide?
options: Study it out, pray sincerely, and trust the feelings the Spirit gives you
Just pick whichever your friends choose
Flip a coin and move on
Wait for someone else to tell you what to do

2.tip: Personal revelation often comes after you've done your part to think it through. (D&C 9:8-9)

3.definition: Personal revelation is your right and responsibility — God intends for you to receive direct guidance for your stewardships.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're choosing a career path and receive conflicting advice from trusted people. Everyone seems sure they know what's best for you. How do you navigate this?
3.scenarioYouDo: You're choosing a career path and receive conflicting advice from trusted people. Everyone seems sure they know what's best for you. How do you navigate this?

challenge.scenario: You're choosing a career path and receive conflicting advice from trusted people. Everyone seems sure they know what's best for you. How do you navigate this?
options: Follow whoever gives the most confident advice
Just go with the most profitable option
Avoid making a decision until everyone agrees
Gather counsel from trusted sources, but ultimately seek and trust personal revelation for your own path

3.tip: Others can advise, but revelation for your life comes to you. Learn to trust it. (D&C 8:2-3)

1.definition: The Holy Ghost can be with you all the time to help and comfort you.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You're at a sleepover and feeling homesick. You remember you were confirmed and given the gift of the Holy Ghost. What does that mean for you right now?
1.scenarioYouDo: You're at a sleepover and feeling homesick. You remember you were confirmed and given the gift of the Holy Ghost. What does that mean for you right now?

challenge.scenario: You're at a sleepover and feeling homesick. You remember you were confirmed and given the gift of the Holy Ghost. What does that mean for you right now?
options: The Holy Ghost can comfort you even when you're away from home
It doesn't help with feeling homesick
The Holy Ghost only works inside church buildings

1.tip: The Holy Ghost is like a friend who is always with you — at home, at school, everywhere! (John 14:26)

2.definition: Through baptism and living faithfully, you can have the Holy Ghost as a constant companion.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You notice you feel the Spirit strongly on Sunday but it fades during the week. What might help you keep that feeling?
2.scenarioYouDo: You notice you feel the Spirit strongly on Sunday but it fades during the week. What might help you keep that feeling?

challenge.scenario: You notice you feel the Spirit strongly on Sunday but it fades during the week. What might help you keep that feeling?
options: The Spirit is only meant for Sundays
You can't control when the Spirit comes — it's random
Just try harder on Sundays and don't worry about the rest of the week
Making daily choices to pray, study scriptures, and live faithfully invites the Spirit every day

2.tip: The Holy Ghost doesn't clock in on Sunday and clock out on Monday — your daily choices keep the connection alive. (D&C 121:46)

3.definition: Having the Holy Ghost as a constant companion is one of the greatest blessings of the gospel — and it requires intentional, daily effort to maintain.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're in college and life is busy. You've noticed the Spirit's guidance dimming, but you reason that you're still a good person and attend church. You rarely pray or study scriptures personally anymore. What's the reality?
3.scenarioYouDo: You're in college and life is busy. You've noticed the Spirit's guidance dimming, but you reason that you're still a good person and attend church. You rarely pray or study scriptures personally anymore. What's the reality?

challenge.scenario: You're in college and life is busy. You've noticed the Spirit's guidance dimming, but you reason that you're still a good person and attend church. You rarely pray or study scriptures personally anymore. What's the reality?
options: Being basically good is enough — personal practices don't matter that much
Admit you might be coasting spiritually and recommit to the personal habits that maintain a strong connection with the Spirit
The Spirit's absence is normal in busy seasons — it'll come back on its own
Focus on church attendance and social aspects of the gospel — personal spirituality is overrated

3.tip: The companionship of the Holy Ghost isn't automatic — it's a relationship that requires your daily investment. (3 Nephi 19:9)

1.definition: When you are baptized, you make a promise with Heavenly Father called a covenant.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your friend is getting baptized this Saturday and asks, 'What am I promising?' What do you tell them?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your friend is getting baptized this Saturday and asks, 'What am I promising?' What do you tell them?

challenge.scenario: Your friend is getting baptized this Saturday and asks, 'What am I promising?' What do you tell them?
options: You're promising to follow Jesus and keep His commandments, and Heavenly Father promises to bless you
You're just getting wet — it doesn't mean anything special
You're promising to be perfect from now on

1.tip: Baptism is a two-way promise — you promise to follow Jesus, and God promises to always be with you! (Mosiah 18:10)

2.definition: At baptism, you covenant to take upon yourself the name of Christ, keep His commandments, and serve others — and God promises you His Spirit.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend asks, 'What's the point of baptism if I'm just going to mess up anyway?' What do you say?
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend asks, 'What's the point of baptism if I'm just going to mess up anyway?' What do you say?

challenge.scenario: A friend asks, 'What's the point of baptism if I'm just going to mess up anyway?' What do you say?
options: Good point — maybe wait until you're sure you won't sin
Baptism doesn't really change anything — it's just a tradition
Baptism starts a covenant relationship with God that includes the gift of repentance, so messing up is expected and covered
You only need baptism if you've committed serious sins

2.tip: Baptism doesn't demand perfection — it gives you access to the Savior's help along the way. (2 Nephi 31:17)

3.definition: Baptismal covenants are foundational — they establish your relationship with God and initiate the covenant path that leads back to His presence.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A young adult considering baptism asks, 'Why can't I just have a personal relationship with God without formal covenants?' How do you help them understand?
3.scenarioYouDo: A young adult considering baptism asks, 'Why can't I just have a personal relationship with God without formal covenants?' How do you help them understand?

challenge.scenario: A young adult considering baptism asks, 'Why can't I just have a personal relationship with God without formal covenants?' How do you help them understand?
options: They're right — covenants are optional extras for those who want them
Covenants are just church tradition — they don't really change your relationship with God
Covenants are God's ordained way of binding Himself to you with specific promises — they structure and deepen your relationship with Him in ways that personal belief alone cannot
Explain that baptism is mainly about joining the church community

3.tip: Covenants don't limit your relationship with God — they elevate it. (D&C 84:33-40)

1.definition: Taking the sacrament helps you remember Jesus and renew your promises.
1.scenarioDoneTo: During sacrament meeting, the bread and water come to you. What should you be thinking about?
1.scenarioYouDo: During sacrament meeting, the bread and water come to you. What should you be thinking about?

challenge.scenario: During sacrament meeting, the bread and water come to you. What should you be thinking about?
options: Remembering Jesus and the promises you made at baptism
What you're having for lunch after church
Nothing — it's just bread and water

1.tip: The sacrament is a special time to think about Jesus and start each week fresh! (3 Nephi 18:7)

2.definition: The sacrament gives you a chance each week to renew your covenants and receive the promise of the Spirit.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You had a tough week and made some mistakes. Sunday comes and you wonder if you should take the sacrament. What's the right approach?
2.scenarioYouDo: You had a tough week and made some mistakes. Sunday comes and you wonder if you should take the sacrament. What's the right approach?

challenge.scenario: You had a tough week and made some mistakes. Sunday comes and you wonder if you should take the sacrament. What's the right approach?
options: Skip the sacrament until you've been perfect for a whole week
The sacrament is exactly for moments like this — it's how you renew your covenants and recommit
It doesn't matter whether you take it or not
Only take it if you feel 100% worthy

2.tip: The sacrament isn't a reward for a perfect week — it's the reset that helps you start again. (Moroni 4:3)

3.definition: The sacrament is a weekly opportunity to consciously realign with Christ, renew all your covenants, and receive the renewed promise of the Spirit.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You find yourself going through the motions during the sacrament — not really thinking about it. Weeks blur together and it feels routine. How do you reclaim the power of this ordinance?
3.scenarioYouDo: You find yourself going through the motions during the sacrament — not really thinking about it. Weeks blur together and it feels routine. How do you reclaim the power of this ordinance?

challenge.scenario: You find yourself going through the motions during the sacrament — not really thinking about it. Weeks blur together and it feels routine. How do you reclaim the power of this ordinance?
options: Accept that the sacrament is mainly ceremonial — deep feelings aren't the point
Just try to sit more reverently
Intentionally prepare each week — review your covenants, reflect on the Savior's sacrifice, and come with specific things to repent of and commit to
Switch wards to see if a different environment helps

3.tip: The sacrament is as powerful as you allow it to be — preparation turns routine into revelation. (D&C 59:9)

1.definition: The temple is Heavenly Father's house, and it's a special place to make promises with Him.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your older sibling is going to the temple for the first time. They seem a little nervous. What do you tell them?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your older sibling is going to the temple for the first time. They seem a little nervous. What do you tell them?

challenge.scenario: Your older sibling is going to the temple for the first time. They seem a little nervous. What do you tell them?
options: The temple is scary — good luck!
It's just another church building, no big deal
The temple is Heavenly Father's house — it's a place of love and peace where you make sacred promises

1.tip: The temple is the closest place to heaven on earth — it's full of peace and love! (D&C 109:13)

2.definition: Temple covenants are building blocks of your foundation in Christ.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend asks why Latter-day Saints care so much about temples when you can worship God anywhere. What do you explain?
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend asks why Latter-day Saints care so much about temples when you can worship God anywhere. What do you explain?

challenge.scenario: A friend asks why Latter-day Saints care so much about temples when you can worship God anywhere. What do you explain?
options: You're right — temples aren't that important
Temples are mainly for looking at
Temples are only for people who are already perfect
Temples are where we make the most sacred covenants with God — promises you can't make anywhere else

2.tip: Temples offer blessings that can't be received any other way — they're part of God's plan for you. (D&C 124:40-41)

3.definition: Temple covenants connect you to God's highest blessings and are essential to building an enduring foundation in Christ.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A friend who is active in the Church hasn't pursued a temple recommend and says, 'I have a good relationship with God — I don't need temple covenants right now.' What's a thoughtful response?
3.scenarioYouDo: A friend who is active in the Church hasn't pursued a temple recommend and says, 'I have a good relationship with God — I don't need temple covenants right now.' What's a thoughtful response?

challenge.scenario: A friend who is active in the Church hasn't pursued a temple recommend and says, 'I have a good relationship with God — I don't need temple covenants right now.' What's a thoughtful response?
options: Respect their choice — temple covenants are optional for committed members
Pressure them into going immediately
Admit that the temple experience is overrated
Gently share that temple covenants aren't supplementary — they're central to God's plan and open blessings that can't be received any other way

3.tip: Temple covenants aren't advanced extras — they're essential steps on the path God has outlined for His children. (D&C 131:1-4)

1.definition: Keeping your promises with Heavenly Father helps you feel closer to Him.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You promised to be reverent during church and you really tried hard today. How do you feel?
1.scenarioYouDo: You promised to be reverent during church and you really tried hard today. How do you feel?

challenge.scenario: You promised to be reverent during church and you really tried hard today. How do you feel?
options: Happy and close to Heavenly Father because you kept your promise
Bored — being reverent is no fun
The same as always — keeping promises doesn't change anything

1.tip: When you keep your promises, Heavenly Father keeps His — and that brings you closer together! (D&C 82:10)

2.definition: Covenants bind you to God with real power — they're not just promises but channels through which God's strength flows into your life.
2.scenarioDoneTo: During a difficult time, your mom says, 'I'm relying on the power of my covenants to get through this.' What does she mean?
2.scenarioYouDo: During a difficult time, your mom says, 'I'm relying on the power of my covenants to get through this.' What does she mean?

challenge.scenario: During a difficult time, your mom says, 'I'm relying on the power of my covenants to get through this.' What does she mean?
options: She's being dramatic — covenants don't help with real problems
She should be relying on herself, not on religious promises
Her covenants with God give her access to His strength and help beyond her own ability
Covenants only help with spiritual problems, not real-life challenges

2.tip: Covenants aren't just promises on paper — they're power lines connecting you to heaven. (D&C 84:20)

3.definition: Covenants are the mechanism through which God binds Himself to you and increases His power in your life — they make His promises operational.
3.scenarioDoneTo: In a gospel discussion, someone asks, 'If God loves everyone, why do covenants matter? Couldn't He just bless everyone equally?' How do you respond?
3.scenarioYouDo: In a gospel discussion, someone asks, 'If God loves everyone, why do covenants matter? Couldn't He just bless everyone equally?' How do you respond?

challenge.scenario: In a gospel discussion, someone asks, 'If God loves everyone, why do covenants matter? Couldn't He just bless everyone equally?' How do you respond?
options: They have a point — covenants might be unnecessary if God is all-loving
God does bless everyone, but covenants create a specific, binding relationship that unlocks additional blessings and power He's eager to give
Covenants are mainly about showing God we're loyal
God only helps covenant-keepers — everyone else is on their own

3.tip: Covenants aren't God's barriers — they're His bridges, designed to bring you more of what He wants to give. (Abraham 2:11)

1.definition: There are special steps that bring you closer to Heavenly Father: baptism, the sacrament, and the temple.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your Primary teacher draws a path with stepping stones labeled 'Baptism,' 'Sacrament,' and 'Temple.' What does this path lead to?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your Primary teacher draws a path with stepping stones labeled 'Baptism,' 'Sacrament,' and 'Temple.' What does this path lead to?

challenge.scenario: Your Primary teacher draws a path with stepping stones labeled 'Baptism,' 'Sacrament,' and 'Temple.' What does this path lead to?
options: A treasure chest full of gold
Back to Heavenly Father's presence
The end of church

1.tip: Each covenant is a stepping stone bringing you closer and closer to Heavenly Father! (2 Nephi 31:18)

2.definition: The covenant path — baptism, the sacrament, and the temple — is the way back to Heavenly Father.
2.scenarioDoneTo: Someone says, 'Why do you need all these ordinances? Can't you just believe in Jesus and be saved?' How do you explain the covenant path?
2.scenarioYouDo: Someone says, 'Why do you need all these ordinances? Can't you just believe in Jesus and be saved?' How do you explain the covenant path?

challenge.scenario: Someone says, 'Why do you need all these ordinances? Can't you just believe in Jesus and be saved?' How do you explain the covenant path?
options: Belief is the first step, but Jesus Christ established ordinances and covenants as the way to fully receive His blessings and return to God
They're right — believing is enough
Ordinances are traditions that don't really matter
You only need baptism — everything else is extra

2.tip: Believing in Christ opens the door — covenants and ordinances walk you through it. (Articles of Faith 1:4)

3.definition: The covenant path is God's structured plan to bring you home — each ordinance builds on the last, creating a cumulative foundation in Christ.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A friend who is investigating the Church asks, 'Why do Latter-day Saints have so many ordinances? It feels like a checklist.' How do you help them see it differently?
3.scenarioYouDo: A friend who is investigating the Church asks, 'Why do Latter-day Saints have so many ordinances? It feels like a checklist.' How do you help them see it differently?

challenge.scenario: A friend who is investigating the Church asks, 'Why do Latter-day Saints have so many ordinances? It feels like a checklist.' How do you help them see it differently?
options: Admit it can feel bureaucratic but it's just how the Church works
Tell them to just start with baptism and worry about the rest later
Agree that fewer ordinances would be simpler
Explain that each ordinance builds on the previous one, progressively connecting you to God — it's not a checklist but a journey, with each step deepening your relationship and power from Him

3.tip: The covenant path isn't a checklist — it's a staircase, and each step lifts you higher. (D&C 132:7)

1.definition: Keeping your promises with Heavenly Father brings real blessings.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You try hard to remember Jesus every day, like you promised at baptism. You notice you feel more peaceful and happy. Why?
1.scenarioYouDo: You try hard to remember Jesus every day, like you promised at baptism. You notice you feel more peaceful and happy. Why?

challenge.scenario: You try hard to remember Jesus every day, like you promised at baptism. You notice you feel more peaceful and happy. Why?
options: It's just a coincidence
You're just in a good mood this week
Keeping your covenants brings blessings from Heavenly Father

1.tip: Heavenly Father always keeps His promises — and when you keep yours, the blessings flow! (Mosiah 2:41)

2.definition: Keeping covenants takes effort, but the blessings that come are worth far more than what it costs.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend says, 'Living the gospel takes too much effort — I want to have fun and just be me.' What would you share?
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend says, 'Living the gospel takes too much effort — I want to have fun and just be me.' What would you share?

challenge.scenario: A friend says, 'Living the gospel takes too much effort — I want to have fun and just be me.' What would you share?
options: They're right — the gospel is mostly about sacrifice
Just do the minimum and you'll be fine
Have fun now and get serious about covenants later
Keeping covenants actually brings more joy and freedom, not less — it's worth the effort

2.tip: The gospel isn't about giving up happiness — it's about finding the deepest kind of it. (D&C 14:7)

3.definition: Keeping covenants is not passive — it requires active, daily recommitment, and it produces spiritual power that sustains you through life's hardest moments.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're going through the hardest trial of your life. Someone suggests that maybe your faith isn't working because things are still hard. How do you respond?
3.scenarioYouDo: You're going through the hardest trial of your life. Someone suggests that maybe your faith isn't working because things are still hard. How do you respond?

challenge.scenario: You're going through the hardest trial of your life. Someone suggests that maybe your faith isn't working because things are still hard. How do you respond?
options: Admit they might be right — if God loved you, things wouldn't be this hard
Agree that faith should prevent trials
Share that covenant-keeping doesn't remove trials — it gives you divine power and companionship to endure and grow through them
Take a break from your covenants until life gets easier

3.tip: Covenants don't promise an easy life — they promise you'll never face it alone. (Mosiah 24:14-15)

1.definition: Your neighbor is everyone — not just the people who live near you.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your friend says only the kids on your street are your 'neighbors.' But at church, your teacher asks, 'Who is your neighbor?'
1.scenarioYouDo: Your friend says only the kids on your street are your 'neighbors.' But at church, your teacher asks, 'Who is your neighbor?'

challenge.scenario: Your friend says only the kids on your street are your 'neighbors.' But at church, your teacher asks, 'Who is your neighbor?'
options: Everyone — all people are your neighbors
Only people who live on your street
Only people at your church

1.tip: Jesus taught that everyone is your neighbor — even people you've never met! (Luke 10:29)

2.definition: Loving your neighbor means caring for everyone, including those who are different from you.
2.scenarioDoneTo: In a class discussion, someone argues that 'love your neighbor' only means being nice to people who live near you. Your teacher asks what Jesus really meant.
2.scenarioYouDo: In a class discussion, someone argues that 'love your neighbor' only means being nice to people who live near you. Your teacher asks what Jesus really meant.

challenge.scenario: In a class discussion, someone argues that 'love your neighbor' only means being nice to people who live near you. Your teacher asks what Jesus really meant.
options: Only people who are nice to you
Only people in your community
Everyone, including those who are different from you
Only people of your same faith

2.tip: When Jesus said 'love your neighbor,' He meant everyone — no exceptions. (Matthew 22:39)

3.definition: All people are your brothers and sisters — including those who disagree with you or see the world differently.
3.scenarioDoneTo: An online debate breaks out about whether loving your neighbor means you have to love people whose views you strongly disagree with. Someone asks your opinion.
3.scenarioYouDo: An online debate breaks out about whether loving your neighbor means you have to love people whose views you strongly disagree with. Someone asks your opinion.

challenge.scenario: An online debate breaks out about whether loving your neighbor means you have to love people whose views you strongly disagree with. Someone asks your opinion.
options: Only those who share my values and beliefs
All people, even those who disagree with me or are very different from me
Everyone except people who have offended me personally
Only people I feel comfortable around

3.tip: God's love has no boundaries — and neither should ours. All people are His children. (2 Nephi 26:33)

1.definition: When you help someone, you are also serving Heavenly Father.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You help a younger child pick up their spilled crayons at school. Your friend asks why you bothered. What do you say?
1.scenarioYouDo: You help a younger child pick up their spilled crayons at school. Your friend asks why you bothered. What do you say?

challenge.scenario: You help a younger child pick up their spilled crayons at school. Your friend asks why you bothered. What do you say?
options: It only counts if people notice
You are also serving Heavenly Father
It's only important if adults tell you to

1.tip: Every act of kindness is also an act of service to God. (Mosiah 2:17)

2.definition: Serving God's children is the same as serving Him, because all people are His children.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You volunteer at a food bank and spend all day sorting cans. Nobody thanks you. You wonder if the effort was worth it.
2.scenarioYouDo: You volunteer at a food bank and spend all day sorting cans. Nobody thanks you. You wonder if the effort was worth it.

challenge.scenario: You volunteer at a food bank and spend all day sorting cans. Nobody thanks you. You wonder if the effort was worth it.
options: You are serving God, because all people are His children
It only matters if you get recognized for it
Service is only meaningful on special occasions
You should only serve people who will repay you

2.tip: Service doesn't need an audience. When you serve His children, you serve God. (Mosiah 2:17)

3.definition: True service comes from seeing others as God sees them — as His sons and daughters.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You spend your Saturday helping an elderly neighbor clean their yard. Your friends are all at the beach. Later they ask, 'Why would you waste your weekend like that?'
3.scenarioYouDo: You spend your Saturday helping an elderly neighbor clean their yard. Your friends are all at the beach. Later they ask, 'Why would you waste your weekend like that?'

challenge.scenario: You spend your Saturday helping an elderly neighbor clean their yard. Your friends are all at the beach. Later they ask, 'Why would you waste your weekend like that?'
options: Service is primarily about building your résumé for college applications
Serving others is a waste of time when you have your own problems
Serving God's children is serving Him — true service comes from seeing others as He sees them
Service only counts if it's organized through your church

3.tip: When you serve 'the least of these,' you are serving the Savior Himself. (Matthew 25:40)

1.definition: The two most important commandments are to love God and love your neighbor.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your Primary teacher asks, 'What are the two most important commandments Jesus taught?'
1.scenarioYouDo: Your Primary teacher asks, 'What are the two most important commandments Jesus taught?'

challenge.scenario: Your Primary teacher asks, 'What are the two most important commandments Jesus taught?'
options: Be the smartest person in the room
Always come in first place
Love God and love your neighbor

1.tip: Everything Jesus taught comes back to love — loving God and loving others. (Matthew 22:37-39)

2.definition: Love is at the heart of all God's commandments — loving God and loving your neighbor.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend at school says being a good person is all about following lots of rules. You want to share what Jesus said matters most.
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend at school says being a good person is all about following lots of rules. You want to share what Jesus said matters most.

challenge.scenario: A friend at school says being a good person is all about following lots of rules. You want to share what Jesus said matters most.
options: Follow every rule perfectly without understanding why
Love God with all your heart and love your neighbor as yourself
Be more successful than everyone around you
Memorize the most scriptures

2.tip: Rules matter, but the heart of the gospel is love — for God and for each other. (Matthew 22:37-39)

3.definition: Everything taught in the scriptures and by the prophets connects back to loving God and loving your neighbor.
3.scenarioDoneTo: In a discussion about priorities, someone argues that strict obedience to every rule is what matters most in the gospel. You know there's a deeper foundation.
3.scenarioYouDo: In a discussion about priorities, someone argues that strict obedience to every rule is what matters most in the gospel. You know there's a deeper foundation.

challenge.scenario: In a discussion about priorities, someone argues that strict obedience to every rule is what matters most in the gospel. You know there's a deeper foundation.
options: Strictly follow religious rituals without personal connection
Focus primarily on personal spiritual experiences and less on others
Keep every commandment out of fear of punishment
Love God wholeheartedly, and love your neighbor — everything else flows from these two

3.tip: Obedience matters — but the foundation is love. All commandments hang on these two. (Matthew 22:37-40)

1.definition: Jesus helped people whenever He could, and so can you.
1.scenarioDoneTo: A classmate drops their books in the hallway and no one stops to help. You're on your way to recess.
1.scenarioYouDo: A classmate drops their books in the hallway and no one stops to help. You're on your way to recess.

challenge.scenario: A classmate drops their books in the hallway and no one stops to help. You're on your way to recess.
options: Help them because that's what Jesus would do
Tell them it's not your problem
Walk away and play with your friends instead

1.tip: Small acts of service — like helping someone pick up their things — show real Christlike love. (Mosiah 4:26)

2.definition: Christlike service means acting out of love, not obligation or reward.
2.scenarioDoneTo: Your neighbor is shoveling their driveway alone after a big snowstorm. You don't really know them well and you have homework to do.
2.scenarioYouDo: Your neighbor is shoveling their driveway alone after a big snowstorm. You don't really know them well and you have homework to do.

challenge.scenario: Your neighbor is shoveling their driveway alone after a big snowstorm. You don't really know them well and you have homework to do.
options: Ignore them because it's not your responsibility
Only help if a teacher is watching
Wait for someone else to take care of it
Offer to help, following Jesus Christ's example of serving others

2.tip: You don't need to know someone well to serve them. Christlike service is for everyone. (James 1:27)

3.definition: The most meaningful service often happens quietly, without recognition or reward.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You notice an elderly man at the grocery store struggling to load bags into his car. Nobody else seems to notice. You're in a hurry.
3.scenarioYouDo: You notice an elderly man at the grocery store struggling to load bags into his car. Nobody else seems to notice. You're in a hurry.

challenge.scenario: You notice an elderly man at the grocery store struggling to load bags into his car. Nobody else seems to notice. You're in a hurry.
options: Look for quiet ways to serve, even when no one is watching or keeping score
Serve only when it's convenient and doesn't cost you anything
Post about your service on social media to inspire others, but skip the hard stuff
Focus on your own goals first — you can serve after you're successful

3.tip: The Savior served without making a show of it. Quiet, selfless service is the most powerful kind. (Matthew 6:3-4)

1.definition: You show love by being kind to people, especially when they need a friend.
1.scenarioDoneTo: A new student at school is sitting alone at lunch and looks sad. What do you do?
1.scenarioYouDo: A new student at school is sitting alone at lunch and looks sad. What do you do?

challenge.scenario: A new student at school is sitting alone at lunch and looks sad. What do you do?
options: Tell them you're too busy
Ask if they want to sit with you at lunch
Pretend you don't notice

1.tip: Love isn't just something you feel — it's something you do. A simple invitation can change someone's day. (1 John 3:18)

2.definition: Genuine love means reaching out consistently, not just once.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A classmate who recently moved into your ward seems really lonely. They don't know anyone and sit alone at activities.
2.scenarioYouDo: A classmate who recently moved into your ward seems really lonely. They don't know anyone and sit alone at activities.

challenge.scenario: A classmate who recently moved into your ward seems really lonely. They don't know anyone and sit alone at activities.
options: Tell them they should try harder to make friends
Ignore them because it's not your problem
Sit with them and include them in your group
Say hi once and then go back to your own friends

2.tip: One hello is a start, but consistent friendship makes someone truly feel they belong. (Mosiah 18:8-9)

3.definition: Love is expressed through consistent, genuine friendship — not just occasional gestures.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A coworker mentions they've been feeling isolated since moving to your area. They don't have family nearby and don't seem to have many friends.
3.scenarioYouDo: A coworker mentions they've been feeling isolated since moving to your area. They don't have family nearby and don't seem to have many friends.

challenge.scenario: A coworker mentions they've been feeling isolated since moving to your area. They don't have family nearby and don't seem to have many friends.
options: Assume someone else will help them
Consistently reach out with genuine friendship, not just a one-time gesture
Include them only if your other friends approve
Be friendly at church but ignore them at school

3.tip: People know you're a disciple of Christ by how you love — and real love is ongoing, not occasional. (John 13:34-35)

1.definition: When you see someone who is alone, you can help them feel included.
1.scenarioDoneTo: At recess, you see a kid sitting alone on a bench while everyone else is playing. What should you do?
1.scenarioYouDo: At recess, you see a kid sitting alone on a bench while everyone else is playing. What should you do?

challenge.scenario: At recess, you see a kid sitting alone on a bench while everyone else is playing. What should you do?
options: Only play with your best friends
Wait for the teacher to tell you to be nice
Go over and invite them to join you

1.tip: Don't wait for someone else to include the lonely person — you can be the one who makes their day. (Alma 34:28)

2.definition: Reach out to those who feel lonely or isolated and help them feel Heavenly Father's love.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A student in your class is always alone at lunch and never gets invited to hang out. You notice they look really sad.
2.scenarioYouDo: A student in your class is always alone at lunch and never gets invited to hang out. You notice they look really sad.

challenge.scenario: A student in your class is always alone at lunch and never gets invited to hang out. You notice they look really sad.
options: Reach out and help them feel included — they need to feel Heavenly Father's love
Hope someone else notices and does something
Assume they want to be alone
Only talk to them if they talk to you first

2.tip: People who feel invisible need someone to see them. You can be that person. (Matthew 25:35-36)

3.definition: Actively look for people who feel isolated or helpless, and help them feel Heavenly Father's love through you.
3.scenarioDoneTo: In your college ward, a new member who seems shy hasn't come back after their first week. Others assume they just aren't interested.
3.scenarioYouDo: In your college ward, a new member who seems shy hasn't come back after their first week. Others assume they just aren't interested.

challenge.scenario: In your college ward, a new member who seems shy hasn't come back after their first week. Others assume they just aren't interested.
options: Assume they prefer being alone since they haven't spoken up
Wait until they ask for help before getting involved
Actively look for people who feel isolated and help them feel Heavenly Father's love through you
Feel bad for them but decide there's nothing you can really do

3.tip: Don't assume someone who disappears is fine. Reach out — your effort could be the answer to their prayer. (Mosiah 18:8-9)

1.definition: Your words should be kind and should never hurt others, even as a joke.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your friend starts making fun of another kid's clothes. Everyone is laughing. What do you do?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your friend starts making fun of another kid's clothes. Everyone is laughing. What do you do?

challenge.scenario: Your friend starts making fun of another kid's clothes. Everyone is laughing. What do you do?
options: Say something kind instead because words matter
It's fine since everyone does it
Ignore it because it's just a joke

1.tip: Your words are powerful. Use them to build people up, never to tear them down. (Ephesians 4:29)

2.definition: Make sure your language reflects love — nothing divisive, hurtful, or offensive, even as a joke.
2.scenarioDoneTo: In your group chat, friends start making hurtful jokes about another student. They say it's 'just a joke.'
2.scenarioYouDo: In your group chat, friends start making hurtful jokes about another student. They say it's 'just a joke.'

challenge.scenario: In your group chat, friends start making hurtful jokes about another student. They say it's 'just a joke.'
options: Laugh and join in — it's just for fun
Speak up or refuse to join in, because your words should uplift, not tear down
Stay quiet so you don't cause drama
Share the joke with more people online

2.tip: Something being 'just a joke' doesn't mean it doesn't hurt. Choose words that uplift. (Proverbs 15:1)

3.definition: Your words can be powerful for good — let your language always reflect love of God and others.
3.scenarioDoneTo: At a party, your friends start making jokes that are hurtful toward a specific group of people. Everyone is laughing. Someone asks if you think it's funny.
3.scenarioYouDo: At a party, your friends start making jokes that are hurtful toward a specific group of people. Everyone is laughing. Someone asks if you think it's funny.

challenge.scenario: At a party, your friends start making jokes that are hurtful toward a specific group of people. Everyone is laughing. Someone asks if you think it's funny.
options: Participate to fit in — social pressure is hard to resist
Stay quiet and avoid the situation entirely
Laugh along but privately feel bad about it
Decline to participate and redirect the conversation — your words should always reflect love

3.tip: It takes courage to redirect a conversation, but your words have the power to change the tone for good. (D&C 121:41-42)

1.definition: Loving God's children starts in your own home with your own family.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your little sister wants you to play with her, but you'd rather watch TV. What's the best choice?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your little sister wants you to play with her, but you'd rather watch TV. What's the best choice?

challenge.scenario: Your little sister wants you to play with her, but you'd rather watch TV. What's the best choice?
options: Let your parents do all the work
Be kind and helpful to your family — love starts at home
Only be nice when you want something

1.tip: Being kind to your family is one of the best ways to practice being like Jesus. (Mosiah 4:15)

2.definition: Do your part to make your home a place where everyone can feel the Savior's love.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You're frustrated because your younger sibling keeps coming into your room. You're tempted to yell at them.
2.scenarioYouDo: You're frustrated because your younger sibling keeps coming into your room. You're tempted to yell at them.

challenge.scenario: You're frustrated because your younger sibling keeps coming into your room. You're tempted to yell at them.
options: Focus on friendships outside the home since family is always there
Only be kind when your parents are watching
Let siblings figure things out on their own
Do your part to make your home a place where everyone feels the Savior's love

2.tip: How you treat your family at home reflects who you really are. Make home a place of love. (Ephesians 4:32)

3.definition: Loving all of God's children starts at home — creating a home where the Savior's love is felt.
3.scenarioDoneTo: Your family has been going through a tough time. There's a lot of tension at home, and it would be easy to just retreat to your room every evening.
3.scenarioYouDo: Your family has been going through a tough time. There's a lot of tension at home, and it would be easy to just retreat to your room every evening.

challenge.scenario: Your family has been going through a tough time. There's a lot of tension at home, and it would be easy to just retreat to your room every evening.
options: Actively contribute to a loving home environment, even when it's hard
Avoid family conflicts by staying in your room most of the time
Focus your energy on friends since family relationships will always be there
Wait until you're an adult to worry about family dynamics

3.tip: You can't always control the mood at home, but you can always contribute love, patience, and kindness. (D&C 88:119)

1.definition: You should build friendships with many different people.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You have one best friend and they say you shouldn't play with anyone else. What should you do?
1.scenarioYouDo: You have one best friend and they say you shouldn't play with anyone else. What should you do?

challenge.scenario: You have one best friend and they say you shouldn't play with anyone else. What should you do?
options: Only have one best friend
Be friends only with people who like the same things
Build friendships with lots of different people

1.tip: Having lots of friends is a wonderful thing! You can be a good friend to many people. (Proverbs 17:17)

2.definition: While you are young, build good friendships with many people.
2.scenarioDoneTo: Your best friend gets upset when you hang out with other people. They say a real best friend only needs one friend.
2.scenarioYouDo: Your best friend gets upset when you hang out with other people. They say a real best friend only needs one friend.

challenge.scenario: Your best friend gets upset when you hang out with other people. They say a real best friend only needs one friend.
options: Build good friendships with many people while you're young
Stick with only one close friend and avoid everyone else
Only be friends with people who are exactly like you
Focus only on friends who are popular

2.tip: Good friends don't limit you. Building friendships with many people helps you grow. (Proverbs 13:20)

3.definition: Intentionally build friendships with a variety of people who help you keep your commitments to Jesus Christ.
3.scenarioDoneTo: Your close friend group resists whenever you try to include new people. They prefer to keep the group small and exclusive.
3.scenarioYouDo: Your close friend group resists whenever you try to include new people. They prefer to keep the group small and exclusive.

challenge.scenario: Your close friend group resists whenever you try to include new people. They prefer to keep the group small and exclusive.
options: Limit your social circle to people who share all your opinions
Invest only in friendships that benefit you socially
Intentionally build friendships with a wide variety of people who help you grow
Avoid close friendships because people always let you down

3.tip: The best friendships are wide and deep — they help you become more like the Savior. (Ecclesiastes 4:9-10)

1.definition: Group activities are a great way to make friends and have fun.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your church class is planning a fun group activity on Saturday. What's a great way to get to know other kids?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your church class is planning a fun group activity on Saturday. What's a great way to get to know other kids?

challenge.scenario: Your church class is planning a fun group activity on Saturday. What's a great way to get to know other kids?
options: Join a fun group activity where everyone can be included
Only hang out with one person at a time
Stay home and don't spend time with friends

1.tip: Group activities are the perfect way to make new friends and have a blast together! (1 Thessalonians 5:11)

2.definition: The best way to get to know others is through wholesome group activities.
2.scenarioDoneTo: Your friend wants to pair off and spend all their time with just one person at every youth activity. They think group activities are boring.
