2.scenarioYouDo: Your friend wants to pair off and spend all their time with just one person at every youth activity. They think group activities are boring.

challenge.scenario: Your friend wants to pair off and spend all their time with just one person at every youth activity. They think group activities are boring.
options: Pair off with just one person every time
Avoid spending time with the opposite sex entirely
Get to know others through wholesome group activities
Only spend time alone with someone you have a crush on

2.tip: Group activities give you a chance to build real friendships without pressure. (Mosiah 18:21)

3.definition: One-on-one dating should wait until you're mature — group activities are the best way to build friendships now.
3.scenarioDoneTo: Your friends keep pressuring you to pair off and go on one-on-one dates, even though you're only 14. They say group activities are 'childish.'
3.scenarioYouDo: Your friends keep pressuring you to pair off and go on one-on-one dates, even though you're only 14. They say group activities are 'childish.'

challenge.scenario: Your friends keep pressuring you to pair off and go on one-on-one dates, even though you're only 14. They say group activities are 'childish.'
options: Rush into pairing off because everyone else seems to be doing it
Build friendships through group activities — one-on-one dating can wait until you're more mature
Avoid all social situations to stay safe
Date as many people as possible to figure out what you want

3.tip: There's nothing childish about group activities. They're where lasting, pressure-free friendships are built. (Proverbs 3:5-6)

1.definition: It's okay to wait until you're older to start dating. Group activities are great right now!
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your older cousin says you should start having a boyfriend or girlfriend right away. But your parents say you're too young.
1.scenarioYouDo: Your older cousin says you should start having a boyfriend or girlfriend right away. But your parents say you're too young.

challenge.scenario: Your older cousin says you should start having a boyfriend or girlfriend right away. But your parents say you're too young.
options: Start dating as soon as possible
Wait until you're older and more mature — group activities are great for now
Never spend time with other kids

1.tip: There's a time for everything. Right now, focus on having fun with lots of friends! (Ecclesiastes 3:1)

2.definition: One-on-one dating should be postponed until you are mature — age 16 is a good guideline.
2.scenarioDoneTo: Some of your friends at school are already pairing off into couples at age 13. They make fun of you for not having a boyfriend or girlfriend yet.
2.scenarioYouDo: Some of your friends at school are already pairing off into couples at age 13. They make fun of you for not having a boyfriend or girlfriend yet.

challenge.scenario: Some of your friends at school are already pairing off into couples at age 13. They make fun of you for not having a boyfriend or girlfriend yet.
options: Start one-on-one dating as soon as you feel ready, at any age
Wait until you're at least 16 for one-on-one dates — group activities are better for now
Never get to know people of the opposite sex
Only spend time with people your parents choose for you

2.tip: There's no rush. Waiting until you're ready protects your heart and helps you grow. (Proverbs 4:23)

3.definition: Save exclusive relationships for when you are older. Build a solid foundation of friendships first.
3.scenarioDoneTo: Several of your friends started dating exclusively at 14 and are in serious relationships. They encourage you to do the same so you're not 'left behind.'
3.scenarioYouDo: Several of your friends started dating exclusively at 14 and are in serious relationships. They encourage you to do the same so you're not 'left behind.'

challenge.scenario: Several of your friends started dating exclusively at 14 and are in serious relationships. They encourage you to do the same so you're not 'left behind.'
options: Jump into exclusive relationships early so you don't miss out
Avoid all social interaction with the opposite sex until marriage
Date one-on-one as early as possible to gain experience
Postpone exclusive relationships until you're older and focus on building a solid foundation of friendships

3.tip: You're not falling behind by waiting. A strong foundation of friendships prepares you for meaningful relationships later. (1 Corinthians 13:11)

1.definition: Spending time with your family and helping each other makes your family stronger.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your little brother asks you to help him with his homework, but your favorite show is about to start.
1.scenarioYouDo: Your little brother asks you to help him with his homework, but your favorite show is about to start.

challenge.scenario: Your little brother asks you to help him with his homework, but your favorite show is about to start.
options: Tell your sibling to go away
Ignore them when they need help
Offer to help them first and watch your show later

1.tip: Helping your family, even in small ways, shows real love. (Mosiah 4:15)

2.definition: Strong families are built by spending meaningful time together and supporting each other.
2.scenarioDoneTo: Your parents suggest a family game night, but you'd rather hang out with friends. It's been a while since your family did something together.
2.scenarioYouDo: Your parents suggest a family game night, but you'd rather hang out with friends. It's been a while since your family did something together.

challenge.scenario: Your parents suggest a family game night, but you'd rather hang out with friends. It's been a while since your family did something together.
options: Argue with your siblings to prove you're right
Spend all your time alone in your room
Ignore family activities to hang out with friends instead
Spend meaningful time together and support each other

2.tip: Family time isn't boring — it's where some of life's best memories are made. (Ephesians 4:32)

3.definition: Investing in family relationships now strengthens bonds that will last forever.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You realize your relationship with your siblings has become distant. Everyone is busy with their own lives and you barely talk anymore.
3.scenarioYouDo: You realize your relationship with your siblings has become distant. Everyone is busy with their own lives and you barely talk anymore.

challenge.scenario: You realize your relationship with your siblings has become distant. Everyone is busy with their own lives and you barely talk anymore.
options: Prioritize family relationships and invest time in strengthening those bonds
Family time is less important now that you have your own social life
Let your parents handle all of the emotional work in the family
Avoid vulnerable conversations — just keep things surface-level at home

3.tip: Family bonds don't maintain themselves. It takes intentional effort — and it's always worth it. (D&C 88:123)

1.definition: People in your ward are like family too, and you can be kind to all of them.
1.scenarioDoneTo: At church, you see an older person sitting alone. Your friends are playing in the hall. What do you do?
1.scenarioYouDo: At church, you see an older person sitting alone. Your friends are playing in the hall. What do you do?

challenge.scenario: At church, you see an older person sitting alone. Your friends are playing in the hall. What do you do?
options: Be friendly and kind to people in your ward — they are like family too
Only talk to kids your same age at church
Sit in the corner and play on a phone

1.tip: Your ward is like a second family. Saying hello to someone can brighten their whole day. (D&C 38:27)

2.definition: Build relationships within your ward family and look for ways to serve.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A new family moves into your ward and their kids seem really shy. They don't know anyone yet.
2.scenarioYouDo: A new family moves into your ward and their kids seem really shy. They don't know anyone yet.

challenge.scenario: A new family moves into your ward and their kids seem really shy. They don't know anyone yet.
options: Only interact with people you already know at church
Skip church activities because they're boring
Build relationships within your ward family and look for ways to serve
Go to church but avoid talking to anyone new

2.tip: New people need someone to make the first move. Be that person — it can change everything. (Galatians 6:10)

3.definition: Your ward family can be a powerful source of support, friendship, and spiritual growth.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You've moved to a new area and don't know anyone in your new ward. It's tempting to skip church and just spend time with school friends.
3.scenarioYouDo: You've moved to a new area and don't know anyone in your new ward. It's tempting to skip church and just spend time with school friends.

challenge.scenario: You've moved to a new area and don't know anyone in your new ward. It's tempting to skip church and just spend time with school friends.
options: Attend church out of obligation without building real connections
Distance yourself from ward members who aren't in your friend group
Invest in your ward family as a source of support, friendship, and spiritual growth
Only engage at church when it directly benefits you

3.tip: Ward families are meant to lift and support you. Invest in those relationships — they'll bless your life. (Moroni 6:5)

1.definition: Learning to be kind and loving now helps you become a great family member later.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your teacher asks, 'Why is it important to be kind now, even though you're just a kid?'
1.scenarioYouDo: Your teacher asks, 'Why is it important to be kind now, even though you're just a kid?'

challenge.scenario: Your teacher asks, 'Why is it important to be kind now, even though you're just a kid?'
options: Families don't matter very much
Learning to be kind now helps you be a good family member later
You don't need to practice being kind until you're grown up

1.tip: Every kind thing you do now is practice for the amazing person you're becoming. (Mosiah 4:15)

2.definition: Now is the time to develop Christlike qualities that will strengthen your future family.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You wonder why your parents encourage you to practice kindness and patience at home. It doesn't seem related to your future.
2.scenarioYouDo: You wonder why your parents encourage you to practice kindness and patience at home. It doesn't seem related to your future.

challenge.scenario: You wonder why your parents encourage you to practice kindness and patience at home. It doesn't seem related to your future.
options: Now is the time to develop Christlike qualities that will bless your future family
Planning for a family is something you only think about as an adult
Your future family will just work out without preparation
The habits you form now have nothing to do with your future

2.tip: The patience, kindness, and love you practice today are building blocks for your future. (D&C 93:40)

3.definition: Prepare now to build your own family founded on the teachings of Jesus Christ.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A friend says there's no point worrying about family values now because that's 'adult stuff.' They think you should just focus on having fun.
3.scenarioYouDo: A friend says there's no point worrying about family values now because that's 'adult stuff.' They think you should just focus on having fun.

challenge.scenario: A friend says there's no point worrying about family values now because that's 'adult stuff.' They think you should just focus on having fun.
options: There's no point thinking about family life until you're actually engaged
Prepare now by developing patience, kindness, and Christlike attributes that will shape your future family
Your family culture will just come naturally without any effort
Focus entirely on career goals — family will sort itself out

3.tip: Your future family is being shaped right now — by the habits, values, and character you're building today. (Proverbs 22:6)

cards.name: Everyone Is a Child of God

1.definition: Every single person is a child of God and deserves to be treated with love.
1.scenarioDoneTo: A classmate says that some people don't deserve to be treated nicely. What do you think?
1.scenarioYouDo: A classmate says that some people don't deserve to be treated nicely. What do you think?

challenge.scenario: A classmate says that some people don't deserve to be treated nicely. What do you think?
options: Only some people are special to God
God only loves people who go to church
Every person is a child of God and deserves love and respect

1.tip: God made every person. That means every person matters — including you! (Acts 17:29)

2.definition: Every person is a child of God, regardless of their background, and deserves love and respect.
2.scenarioDoneTo: Someone at school says certain people are less important because of where they come from. How would you respond?
2.scenarioYouDo: Someone at school says certain people are less important because of where they come from. How would you respond?

challenge.scenario: Someone at school says certain people are less important because of where they come from. How would you respond?
options: Only people of your faith deserve respect
Every person is a child of God, so treat everyone with love and respect
Some people don't deserve to be treated well
You only need to be kind to people who are kind to you

2.tip: No one's worth is determined by where they come from. In God's eyes, every person matters. (Acts 10:34-35)

3.definition: All people are children of God — treating everyone with dignity is a core part of discipleship.
3.scenarioDoneTo: In an online discussion, someone argues that only people who share their beliefs deserve respect and kindness. How do you respond?
3.scenarioYouDo: In an online discussion, someone argues that only people who share their beliefs deserve respect and kindness. How do you respond?

challenge.scenario: In an online discussion, someone argues that only people who share their beliefs deserve respect and kindness. How do you respond?
options: People who don't share your beliefs don't deserve the same respect
You can choose to respect only people who earn it
Being a child of God only applies to members of your own faith
All people are children of God — treat everyone with dignity regardless of their background or beliefs

3.tip: Dignity isn't earned — it's inherent. Every person carries divine worth as a child of God. (D&C 38:24-25)

1.definition: If someone is being bullied, tell a trusted adult and try to help.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You see an older kid picking on a younger student on the playground. The younger student looks scared.
1.scenarioYouDo: You see an older kid picking on a younger student on the playground. The younger student looks scared.

challenge.scenario: You see an older kid picking on a younger student on the playground. The younger student looks scared.
options: Tell a trusted adult and try to help the person being bullied
Watch but don't get involved
Join in so you don't become the target

1.tip: Speaking up for someone who is being hurt is one of the bravest things you can do. (Proverbs 31:8-9)

2.definition: Set healthy boundaries against bullying — speak up and involve a trusted adult.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You notice a student in your class is being cyberbullied in a group chat. Others are either participating or staying silent.
2.scenarioYouDo: You notice a student in your class is being cyberbullied in a group chat. Others are either participating or staying silent.

challenge.scenario: You notice a student in your class is being cyberbullied in a group chat. Others are either participating or staying silent.
options: Mind your own business — it's not your problem
Bully them back to teach them a lesson
Stay quiet so you don't become a target
Speak up, support the person being hurt, and talk to a trusted adult

2.tip: Silence in the face of bullying is not neutrality — it's permission. Your voice matters. (D&C 121:41-42)

3.definition: If you or someone else is being bullied or abused, set healthy boundaries and talk to a trustworthy adult.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A friend confides that they've been experiencing bullying at school for months. They feel helpless and have told no one else.
3.scenarioYouDo: A friend confides that they've been experiencing bullying at school for months. They feel helpless and have told no one else.

challenge.scenario: A friend confides that they've been experiencing bullying at school for months. They feel helpless and have told no one else.
options: Take action by supporting the victim and involving a trusted adult — set healthy boundaries against bullying
Ignore it — getting involved will only make things worse
Wait for authorities to notice on their own
Confront the bully aggressively to make them stop

3.tip: When someone trusts you with their pain, take it seriously. Help them find safety and support. (Mosiah 18:8-9)

1.definition: Jesus taught us to be kind to everyone, even people who are mean to us.
1.scenarioDoneTo: A kid at school said something mean to you at recess. Now they need help with something. What do you do?
1.scenarioYouDo: A kid at school said something mean to you at recess. Now they need help with something. What do you do?

challenge.scenario: A kid at school said something mean to you at recess. Now they need help with something. What do you do?
options: Be mean back to them
Be kind and pray for them, even when it's hard
Ignore them forever

1.tip: Being kind to someone who was mean to you is hard — but it's exactly what Jesus would do. (Matthew 5:44)

2.definition: The Lord expects you to love your enemies and pray for those who mistreat you.
2.scenarioDoneTo: Someone who has been really unkind to you all year is struggling in class and asks you for help. You feel conflicted.
2.scenarioYouDo: Someone who has been really unkind to you all year is struggling in class and asks you for help. You feel conflicted.

challenge.scenario: Someone who has been really unkind to you all year is struggling in class and asks you for help. You feel conflicted.
options: Plot revenge to get even
Gossip about them to your friends
Pray for them and treat them with kindness, as Jesus taught
Pretend they don't exist

2.tip: Loving your enemies doesn't mean what they did was okay. It means you choose to respond with Christlike love. (Luke 6:27-28)

3.definition: Love your enemies and pray for those who mistreat you — while setting healthy boundaries to protect yourself.
3.scenarioDoneTo: Someone who spread hurtful rumors about you reaches out asking for forgiveness. You're still hurt by what they did.
3.scenarioYouDo: Someone who spread hurtful rumors about you reaches out asking for forgiveness. You're still hurt by what they did.

challenge.scenario: Someone who spread hurtful rumors about you reaches out asking for forgiveness. You're still hurt by what they did.
options: Cut them out of your life completely and hold a grudge
Choose to love and pray for those who mistreat you, while still setting healthy boundaries
Retaliate in subtle ways so they know how it feels
Suppress your feelings and pretend nothing happened

3.tip: Forgiveness and boundaries can coexist. You can love someone and still protect your peace. (3 Nephi 12:44)

1.definition: Be kind and respectful to everyone, no matter how different they are from you.
1.scenarioDoneTo: A new student at school looks different from everyone else and speaks another language. Some kids avoid them.
1.scenarioYouDo: A new student at school looks different from everyone else and speaks another language. Some kids avoid them.

challenge.scenario: A new student at school looks different from everyone else and speaks another language. Some kids avoid them.
options: Only be friends with people who look like you
Avoid people who are different
Be kind and respectful to everyone, no matter how different they are

1.tip: Everyone is different — and that's wonderful! Being kind to everyone is what God asks of us. (2 Nephi 26:33)

2.definition: Treat people of all races and religions with love, respect, and inclusion.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A classmate makes a joke about someone's religion. Others laugh. You know it was hurtful.
2.scenarioYouDo: A classmate makes a joke about someone's religion. Others laugh. You know it was hurtful.

challenge.scenario: A classmate makes a joke about someone's religion. Others laugh. You know it was hurtful.
options: Treat people of all races and religions with love, respect, and inclusion
Only respect people who believe the same things you do
Be polite but avoid building real relationships with people who are different
It's okay to make fun of someone's culture if you're just joking

2.tip: No religion, race, or culture is a punchline. Respect means honoring what matters to others. (Acts 17:26)

3.definition: As a disciple of Christ, lead out in treating people of all races, religions, and groups with genuine respect and inclusion.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're at a new job and your coworkers often make offhand comments about people from different cultural backgrounds. No one else seems bothered.
3.scenarioYouDo: You're at a new job and your coworkers often make offhand comments about people from different cultural backgrounds. No one else seems bothered.

challenge.scenario: You're at a new job and your coworkers often make offhand comments about people from different cultural backgrounds. No one else seems bothered.
options: Respect only those cultures and religions you personally agree with
Be tolerant privately but avoid public displays of inclusion
Lead out in treating people of all races, religions, and backgrounds with genuine respect and inclusion
Respect diversity in theory but stay in your comfort zone socially

3.tip: Disciples of Christ don't just tolerate differences — they actively lead out in respect and inclusion. (D&C 38:24-25)

1.definition: If someone keeps hurting you, tell an adult you trust so you can stay safe.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Another kid keeps saying hurtful things to you every day at school. You've asked them to stop, but they won't.
1.scenarioYouDo: Another kid keeps saying hurtful things to you every day at school. You've asked them to stop, but they won't.

challenge.scenario: Another kid keeps saying hurtful things to you every day at school. You've asked them to stop, but they won't.
options: Tell an adult you trust and stay safe
Do nothing because it might get worse
Fight back on your own

1.tip: It's smart and brave to ask for help when someone is hurting you. (Proverbs 22:3)

2.definition: Loving others doesn't mean letting them hurt you — set healthy boundaries and talk to a trusted adult.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend regularly puts you down in front of others and says they're 'just joking.' When you tell them it hurts, they say you're too sensitive.
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend regularly puts you down in front of others and says they're 'just joking.' When you tell them it hurts, they say you're too sensitive.

challenge.scenario: A friend regularly puts you down in front of others and says they're 'just joking.' When you tell them it hurts, they say you're too sensitive.
options: Suffer in silence because asking for help is weakness
Set healthy boundaries and talk to a trustworthy adult to keep yourself safe
Handle it all on your own — adults won't understand
Ignore the problem and hope it goes away

2.tip: A real friend doesn't make you feel bad about yourself. Setting boundaries isn't unkind — it's wise. (2 Timothy 1:7)

3.definition: Loving your enemies does not mean accepting abuse — set healthy boundaries to keep yourself safe.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A close friend frequently manipulates you emotionally and crosses your boundaries. When you push back, they guilt-trip you and say you're not being Christlike.
3.scenarioYouDo: A close friend frequently manipulates you emotionally and crosses your boundaries. When you push back, they guilt-trip you and say you're not being Christlike.

challenge.scenario: A close friend frequently manipulates you emotionally and crosses your boundaries. When you push back, they guilt-trip you and say you're not being Christlike.
options: Tolerate harmful behavior to avoid conflict
Handle everything internally — asking for help means you're not strong enough
Stay in harmful situations because you feel obligated to forgive and move on
Recognize that loving others doesn't mean accepting abuse — set healthy boundaries and seek help from trusted adults

3.tip: Christlike love includes protecting yourself. Healthy boundaries and forgiveness are not opposites. (D&C 121:41-43)

1.definition: When people argue, you can help by being kind and encouraging everyone to get along.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Two of your friends are in an argument and both want you to take their side. What do you do?
1.scenarioYouDo: Two of your friends are in an argument and both want you to take their side. What do you do?

challenge.scenario: Two of your friends are in an argument and both want you to take their side. What do you do?
options: Pick a side and argue for them
Try to help everyone calm down and be kind
Walk away and ignore it

1.tip: Peacemakers don't pick sides — they help everyone feel heard and loved. (Matthew 5:9)

2.definition: Blessed are the peacemakers — work to bring understanding and kindness in times of conflict.
2.scenarioDoneTo: Two groups of friends at school are feuding and the tension is spreading. People are picking sides.
2.scenarioYouDo: Two groups of friends at school are feuding and the tension is spreading. People are picking sides.

challenge.scenario: Two groups of friends at school are feuding and the tension is spreading. People are picking sides.
options: Take sides to make it end faster
Stir things up because the drama is interesting
Stay out of it completely — it's not your problem
Help both sides feel heard and encourage kindness and understanding

2.tip: Peacemaking is active — it means stepping into conflict with empathy, not stepping away. (3 Nephi 12:9)

3.definition: A disciple of Christ actively works to bring peace through empathy, understanding, and Christlike love.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A heated conflict breaks out in your friend group over a social media post. People are saying hurtful things and relationships are fracturing.
3.scenarioYouDo: A heated conflict breaks out in your friend group over a social media post. People are saying hurtful things and relationships are fracturing.

challenge.scenario: A heated conflict breaks out in your friend group over a social media post. People are saying hurtful things and relationships are fracturing.
options: Actively work to bring peace by encouraging understanding, empathy, and Christlike love
Avoid conflict at all costs, even if it means ignoring injustice
Pick the side that seems more righteous and defend them
Stay neutral and detached — peacemaking is someone else's job

3.tip: Peacemaking isn't passive. It takes courage to bring people together when it's easier to walk away. (D&C 121:41-42)

1.definition: What you watch, read, and listen to affects how you feel inside.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You're looking for a show to watch after school. One show has lots of mean humor and bad words, and another is a fun adventure that makes you feel happy.
1.scenarioYouDo: You're looking for a show to watch after school. One show has lots of mean humor and bad words, and another is a fun adventure that makes you feel happy.

challenge.scenario: You're looking for a show to watch after school. One show has lots of mean humor and bad words, and another is a fun adventure that makes you feel happy.
options: Watch whatever your friends are watching so you fit in
Pick shows and songs that make you feel happy and closer to Heavenly Father
Only watch church videos and nothing else

1.tip: The things you let into your mind shape how you feel. Choose things that bring good feelings! (Philippians 4:8)

2.definition: Your mind and spirit are deeply affected by the media you consume. Seek what uplifts and invites the Spirit.
2.scenarioDoneTo: Your friends are all watching a popular new series that has a lot of crude humor and inappropriate scenes. They keep quoting it at school and you feel left out.
2.scenarioYouDo: Your friends are all watching a popular new series that has a lot of crude humor and inappropriate scenes. They keep quoting it at school and you feel left out.

challenge.scenario: Your friends are all watching a popular new series that has a lot of crude humor and inappropriate scenes. They keep quoting it at school and you feel left out.
options: It doesn't matter what you watch as long as you're a good person
Only consume media made by Church members
Choose media that uplifts your spirit and invites good feelings
Avoid all media entirely to be safe

2.tip: You can enjoy great entertainment and still choose things that leave you feeling uplifted rather than drained. (D&C 88:118)

3.definition: Seeking media that inspires, enlightens, and invites the Spirit is a form of personal discipleship.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A critically acclaimed film everyone is raving about contains graphic content and mocks religious belief. A friend argues you need to watch it to be 'culturally literate.'
3.scenarioYouDo: A critically acclaimed film everyone is raving about contains graphic content and mocks religious belief. A friend argues you need to watch it to be 'culturally literate.'

challenge.scenario: A critically acclaimed film everyone is raving about contains graphic content and mocks religious belief. A friend argues you need to watch it to be 'culturally literate.'
options: Seek out media that inspires you, strengthens your faith, and invites the Spirit
Mature content is fine as long as it has an important artistic message
You should only read scriptures and nothing else for entertainment
What you consume doesn't really affect your spirituality if your testimony is strong

3.tip: Discipleship includes what you choose to fill your mind with. Anything that invites you to do good and believe in Christ is inspired of God. (Moroni 7:16-17)

1.definition: You can be brave and turn something off or walk away if it doesn't feel right.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You're at a friend's house and they start a movie that has scary and bad things in it. You feel uncomfortable.
1.scenarioYouDo: You're at a friend's house and they start a movie that has scary and bad things in it. You feel uncomfortable.

challenge.scenario: You're at a friend's house and they start a movie that has scary and bad things in it. You feel uncomfortable.
options: Turn it off or walk away because something doesn't feel right
Keep watching because it might get better
Wait for a grown-up to come decide for you

1.tip: It takes courage to say 'no' — but you'll always feel proud of yourself when you do. (1 Nephi 15:24)

2.definition: Have the courage to turn off a video, walk out of a movie, or change your music when something conflicts with your values.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You're at a sleepover and everyone is playing a video game filled with extremely graphic violence. You feel uneasy, but you don't want to be the one who ruins the fun.
2.scenarioYouDo: You're at a sleepover and everyone is playing a video game filled with extremely graphic violence. You feel uneasy, but you don't want to be the one who ruins the fun.

challenge.scenario: You're at a sleepover and everyone is playing a video game filled with extremely graphic violence. You feel uneasy, but you don't want to be the one who ruins the fun.
options: You'll seem rude if you leave, so just stay quiet
It's not a big deal since you're only playing it once
Just close your eyes during the worst parts
Have the courage to turn off or step away from content that doesn't feel right

2.tip: Real friends will respect your choices. It takes strength to stand up for what you believe, especially in a group. (Helaman 5:12)

3.definition: True integrity means being willing to remove yourself from media situations that conflict with your standards, even when it's socially costly.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're on a date and the movie turns out to have far more explicit content than the trailer suggested. Your date seems fine with it, and leaving would be awkward.
3.scenarioYouDo: You're on a date and the movie turns out to have far more explicit content than the trailer suggested. Your date seems fine with it, and leaving would be awkward.

challenge.scenario: You're on a date and the movie turns out to have far more explicit content than the trailer suggested. Your date seems fine with it, and leaving would be awkward.
options: Peer pressure means you should just go along to avoid an awkward situation
Be willing to walk out of a movie, change your music, or turn off content that conflicts with your values — regardless of social pressure
Only leave if the content is officially rated for mature audiences
It's fine to stay as long as you mentally disagree with what's being shown

3.tip: Your standards don't take a break because of social pressure. The momentary awkwardness of leaving is far lighter than the weight of compromising who you are. (2 Timothy 2:22)

1.definition: You are special because you are a child of Heavenly Father — not because of likes or followers.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You post a drawing you worked really hard on, but it only gets two likes. Your classmate's photo got over a hundred likes. You start feeling bad about yourself.
1.scenarioYouDo: You post a drawing you worked really hard on, but it only gets two likes. Your classmate's photo got over a hundred likes. You start feeling bad about yourself.

challenge.scenario: You post a drawing you worked really hard on, but it only gets two likes. Your classmate's photo got over a hundred likes. You start feeling bad about yourself.
options: You should try harder to get more likes next time
If someone gets more likes, it means they are better than you
You are special because you are a child of Heavenly Father, not because of likes or followers

1.tip: Likes and followers don't decide how amazing you are. Heavenly Father already thinks you're wonderful! (Psalm 82:6)

2.definition: Your worth comes from being a child of heavenly parents, not from social media popularity or online comparisons.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You spend an hour scrolling through social media and everyone seems to have perfect lives — amazing vacations, tons of friends, flawless photos. You start feeling like your life is boring and you're not enough.
2.scenarioYouDo: You spend an hour scrolling through social media and everyone seems to have perfect lives — amazing vacations, tons of friends, flawless photos. You start feeling like your life is boring and you're not enough.

challenge.scenario: You spend an hour scrolling through social media and everyone seems to have perfect lives — amazing vacations, tons of friends, flawless photos. You start feeling like your life is boring and you're not enough.
options: Your worth comes from being a child of heavenly parents, not from online popularity
You need a big following to matter in today's world
Comparing yourself to others online is a good way to motivate self-improvement
Social media shows what people's lives are really like

2.tip: Social media shows highlight reels, not real life. Your worth was never meant to be measured by a screen. (D&C 18:10)

3.definition: The curated nature of social media can distort self-perception. Your divine identity as a child of God is the true measure of your worth.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You've noticed that your mood and self-confidence are directly tied to how your posts perform. Good engagement means a good day; low engagement spirals you into self-doubt. You realize social media is affecting your mental health.
3.scenarioYouDo: You've noticed that your mood and self-confidence are directly tied to how your posts perform. Good engagement means a good day; low engagement spirals you into self-doubt. You realize social media is affecting your mental health.

challenge.scenario: You've noticed that your mood and self-confidence are directly tied to how your posts perform. Good engagement means a good day; low engagement spirals you into self-doubt. You realize social media is affecting your mental health.
options: Building a personal brand online is essential to your identity in the modern world
Curated online lives are a reasonable standard to measure your own progress against
Social media approval is important for healthy self-esteem and social development
Your divine worth as a child of God doesn't depend on likes, followers, or how your life compares to others online

3.tip: If a screen is shaping how you see yourself, it's time to step back. Your identity was established long before social media existed — in the presence of heavenly parents who love you. (Moses 1:39)

1.definition: Stay away from shows, games, or music that make fun of God, prayer, or other sacred things.
1.scenarioDoneTo: A popular cartoon makes fun of people who pray and go to church. Your friends think it's hilarious.
1.scenarioYouDo: A popular cartoon makes fun of people who pray and go to church. Your friends think it's hilarious.

challenge.scenario: A popular cartoon makes fun of people who pray and go to church. Your friends think it's hilarious.
options: It's funny, so it's okay to laugh along
Stay away from shows that make fun of sacred things like God and prayer
It doesn't matter as long as you still go to church yourself

1.tip: Sacred things deserve respect. When we protect what's holy, we protect the good feelings that come from the Spirit. (D&C 63:64)

2.definition: Avoid media that mocks or disrespects sacred things, including God, the temple, scriptures, and prayer.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A comedy podcast that all your friends listen to frequently makes fun of religion, including jokes about temples and scripture. They say it's all in good fun.
2.scenarioYouDo: A comedy podcast that all your friends listen to frequently makes fun of religion, including jokes about temples and scripture. They say it's all in good fun.

challenge.scenario: A comedy podcast that all your friends listen to frequently makes fun of religion, including jokes about temples and scripture. They say it's all in good fun.
options: A little irreverent humor about religion is normal and harmless
Avoid media that mocks sacred things like God, temples, or prayer
Only avoid it if it specifically targets your church
It's okay if the creator didn't mean to be disrespectful

2.tip: What starts as 'just jokes' can slowly change how you feel about things that really matter. Guard what is sacred to you. (Alma 37:16)

3.definition: Choosing not to consume or support media that mocks the sacred reflects the depth of your reverence for God — even when the world calls it harmless entertainment.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A widely praised TV show satirizes organized religion in a way many find clever and thought-provoking. Colleagues discuss it constantly. Some Church members you respect watch it and say it 'doesn't bother them.'
3.scenarioYouDo: A widely praised TV show satirizes organized religion in a way many find clever and thought-provoking. Colleagues discuss it constantly. Some Church members you respect watch it and say it 'doesn't bother them.'

challenge.scenario: A widely praised TV show satirizes organized religion in a way many find clever and thought-provoking. Colleagues discuss it constantly. Some Church members you respect watch it and say it 'doesn't bother them.'
options: Satire about religion is always harmless artistic expression worth engaging with
You can separate entertainment from personal belief, so it won't affect you
Choose not to support or consume media that mocks or disrespects sacred things, even when others say it's just humor
Only avoid it if it directly and intentionally attacks the Church of Jesus Christ

3.tip: What we laugh at, we eventually become comfortable with. Protecting your reverence for sacred things is worth more than being part of every cultural conversation. (D&C 6:12)

1.definition: Don't let screens take the place of spending time with your family and friends.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your family is having a game night, but you really want to keep playing a game on your tablet. Everyone is waiting for you to join.
1.scenarioYouDo: Your family is having a game night, but you really want to keep playing a game on your tablet. Everyone is waiting for you to join.

challenge.scenario: Your family is having a game night, but you really want to keep playing a game on your tablet. Everyone is waiting for you to join.
options: Put your tablet away and join your family for game night
Keep playing on your tablet since you're still in the same room
Tell your family that game night is boring compared to your game

1.tip: Screens will always be there, but family moments are special. Put the device down and make memories together! (Mosiah 4:15)

2.definition: Be careful that technology doesn't replace quality face-to-face time with family and friends.
2.scenarioDoneTo: At family dinner, everyone is on their phone — including you. Your little sibling tries to tell you about their day, but you're too focused on a group chat to listen.
2.scenarioYouDo: At family dinner, everyone is on their phone — including you. Your little sibling tries to tell you about their day, but you're too focused on a group chat to listen.

challenge.scenario: At family dinner, everyone is on their phone — including you. Your little sibling tries to tell you about their day, but you're too focused on a group chat to listen.
options: Having your phone out during family dinner is fine as long as you eat together
Online conversations are just as meaningful as in-person ones
Be careful that screens don't replace quality face-to-face time with family and friends
You can give equal attention to both your phone and the people around you

2.tip: Being physically present but mentally absent isn't really being there. The people in front of you deserve your full attention. (3 Nephi 18:21)

3.definition: Intentionally guarding in-person relationships from being replaced by technology is essential to strong families and genuine friendships.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You realize you spend more time interacting with people online than you do with your own family in the next room. Your relationships feel shallow, and you can't remember the last deep conversation you had face to face.
3.scenarioYouDo: You realize you spend more time interacting with people online than you do with your own family in the next room. Your relationships feel shallow, and you can't remember the last deep conversation you had face to face.

challenge.scenario: You realize you spend more time interacting with people online than you do with your own family in the next room. Your relationships feel shallow, and you can't remember the last deep conversation you had face to face.
options: Technology has made in-person interaction less necessary than it used to be
Be intentional about not letting technology replace meaningful in-person connections with family and friends
As long as you're texting family, it counts as quality time together
It's unrealistic to try to limit screen time in the modern world

3.tip: Technology is a tool, not a replacement for human connection. The richest relationships are built face to face, not screen to screen. (D&C 93:40)

1.definition: You can use the internet to share kind and uplifting things that help others feel good.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You want to post something online. You could share a mean joke about a kid at school, a random meme, or a kind message about something you're thankful for.
1.scenarioYouDo: You want to post something online. You could share a mean joke about a kid at school, a random meme, or a kind message about something you're thankful for.

challenge.scenario: You want to post something online. You could share a mean joke about a kid at school, a random meme, or a kind message about something you're thankful for.
options: Post the mean joke because it's funny
The internet is only for silly things, not for anything meaningful
Share something kind and uplifting that could make someone else's day better

1.tip: Every post is a chance to brighten someone's day. Use your voice online to spread light! (Matthew 5:16)

2.definition: Use social media to share things that uplift, focus on faith, and bring light and truth to others.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You're setting up your first social media account and thinking about what kind of content you want to be known for. Your friends mostly post about drama and gossip.
2.scenarioYouDo: You're setting up your first social media account and thinking about what kind of content you want to be known for. Your friends mostly post about drama and gossip.

challenge.scenario: You're setting up your first social media account and thinking about what kind of content you want to be known for. Your friends mostly post about drama and gossip.
options: Post whatever gets the most attention and followers
Social media is too dangerous so you should never use it
What you post doesn't really matter because it's just the internet
Use social media to share things that uplift others and focus on faith, light, and truth

2.tip: Your online presence is part of your influence. Make it a place where people come away feeling better, not worse. (D&C 115:5)

3.definition: Social media is a powerful platform to be a light — sharing truth, faith, and positivity in a world that often lacks it.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You have a growing social media following. You could grow faster by posting controversial takes and outrage content, or you could use your platform to share uplifting, faith-centered content that reaches fewer people but genuinely helps them.
3.scenarioYouDo: You have a growing social media following. You could grow faster by posting controversial takes and outrage content, or you could use your platform to share uplifting, faith-centered content that reaches fewer people but genuinely helps them.

challenge.scenario: You have a growing social media following. You could grow faster by posting controversial takes and outrage content, or you could use your platform to share uplifting, faith-centered content that reaches fewer people but genuinely helps them.
options: Use your online presence to uplift, share truth, and be a light — focusing on faith and positivity
Going viral is the most effective way to spread goodness
Avoid social media entirely since it's overwhelmingly negative
Your online presence is just entertainment and doesn't reflect who you really are

3.tip: Reach matters less than impact. One sincere, uplifting post can change someone's day — or even their direction in life. (3 Nephi 12:16)

1.definition: Watching or playing things with lots of violence can make it harder to feel the Holy Ghost.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your older cousin wants to play a video game with you that has a lot of fighting and people getting hurt. It makes you feel uneasy inside.
1.scenarioYouDo: Your older cousin wants to play a video game with you that has a lot of fighting and people getting hurt. It makes you feel uneasy inside.

challenge.scenario: Your older cousin wants to play a video game with you that has a lot of fighting and people getting hurt. It makes you feel uneasy inside.
options: It's totally fine because it's just a game and not real life
Avoid games and shows with lots of violence because they can make it harder to feel the Spirit
Violent games are okay if they're cartoons instead of real people

1.tip: If something makes you feel uneasy inside, that's the Spirit telling you to choose something better. (Moroni 7:12)

2.definition: Don't participate in entertainment that features heavy violence — it can gradually dull your judgment and sensitivity to the Spirit.
2.scenarioDoneTo: All your friends play an extremely violent online game and want you to join their squad. You've noticed that some of them have become more aggressive and desensitized since they started playing it.
2.scenarioYouDo: All your friends play an extremely violent online game and want you to join their squad. You've noticed that some of them have become more aggressive and desensitized since they started playing it.

challenge.scenario: All your friends play an extremely violent online game and want you to join their squad. You've noticed that some of them have become more aggressive and desensitized since they started playing it.
options: Avoid entertainment with heavy violence because it can dull your sensitivity to the Spirit
Violence in media doesn't affect you if you know it's fictional
Only live-action violence is a concern, not animated or video game violence
A little violence is fine as long as the overall story has a good moral

2.tip: What we consume shapes how we feel, think, and react. If media is making you numb to violence, it's time to step away. (D&C 121:45)

3.definition: Repeated exposure to violent media gradually desensitizes your conscience and dulls your ability to receive spiritual promptings.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You've been gaming heavily for months and realize that scenes of suffering that once bothered you now barely register. A violent news story that would have disturbed you a year ago doesn't faze you. You wonder if your media habits are changing you.
3.scenarioYouDo: You've been gaming heavily for months and realize that scenes of suffering that once bothered you now barely register. A violent news story that would have disturbed you a year ago doesn't faze you. You wonder if your media habits are changing you.

challenge.scenario: You've been gaming heavily for months and realize that scenes of suffering that once bothered you now barely register. A violent news story that would have disturbed you a year ago doesn't faze you. You wonder if your media habits are changing you.
options: Consuming violent media is harmless because you would never act that way in real life
It's unrealistic to avoid all violent content in today's media landscape
Be aware that repeated exposure to violence in media can gradually dull your judgment and sensitivity to the Spirit
Only extremely graphic content is spiritually harmful — moderate violence is fine

3.tip: Desensitization happens slowly — you rarely notice it in real time. If the things that once troubled your conscience no longer do, take it seriously. (Alma 39:9)

1.definition: Sunday is a special day to show Heavenly Father you love Him by going to church and resting.
1.scenarioDoneTo: It's Sunday morning and your friends invite you to go to the pool all day. Your family is getting ready for church.
1.scenarioYouDo: It's Sunday morning and your friends invite you to go to the pool all day. Your family is getting ready for church.

challenge.scenario: It's Sunday morning and your friends invite you to go to the pool all day. Your family is getting ready for church.
options: Sunday is a special day to worship Heavenly Father and be with your family
Sunday is just a regular day off from school
You only need to go to church if there's nothing more fun to do

1.tip: Sunday is like a gift — a whole day to focus on Heavenly Father and the people you love most. (Exodus 20:8)

2.definition: Keeping the Sabbath day holy shows God you are willing to dedicate one day each week to Him.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You have a big basketball tournament on Sunday. Your team really wants you there, and your coach says missing it could cost you your starting position.
2.scenarioYouDo: You have a big basketball tournament on Sunday. Your team really wants you there, and your coach says missing it could cost you your starting position.

challenge.scenario: You have a big basketball tournament on Sunday. Your team really wants you there, and your coach says missing it could cost you your starting position.
options: The Sabbath is outdated and doesn't really apply to modern life
Keeping the Sabbath day holy shows God you are willing to dedicate one day each week to Him
The Sabbath is just about sitting in church for an hour
You can keep the Sabbath while doing everything you'd normally do on other days

2.tip: Choosing the Sabbath over other opportunities shows God where your heart is. He honors that kind of devotion. (D&C 59:9)

3.definition: Sabbath observance is an outward expression of your inner covenant relationship with God — a deliberate choice to prioritize the eternal over the immediate.
3.scenarioDoneTo: Your employer offers you a lucrative Sunday shift that would solve your financial stress. Turning it down means continued financial struggle. Friends tell you 'God will understand.'
3.scenarioYouDo: Your employer offers you a lucrative Sunday shift that would solve your financial stress. Turning it down means continued financial struggle. Friends tell you 'God will understand.'

challenge.scenario: Your employer offers you a lucrative Sunday shift that would solve your financial stress. Turning it down means continued financial struggle. Friends tell you 'God will understand.'
options: The Sabbath is mainly about physical rest and recharging for the week
Following the Sabbath is only important when it's convenient
Sunday observance is a personal preference with no real spiritual consequence
Honoring the Sabbath is a sign of your covenant with God to set apart one day a week for worship, service, and drawing closer to Him

3.tip: When you put God first — even at a cost — you're not losing something. You're showing Him you trust His ability to provide what you truly need. (Isaiah 58:13-14)

2.scenarioDoneTo: Your youth group is talking about why God doesn't just force everyone to do the right thing. A friend says it would be easier if He did. What's the best response?
