challenge.scenario: Your youth group is talking about why God doesn't just force everyone to do the right thing. A friend says it would be easier if He did. What's the best response?
options: It would be easier if we didn't have to choose
God doesn't really care what we decide
Agency is a gift because God trusts us to grow through our choices
We only have agency for small decisions

2.tip: Agency isn't a burden — it's a sign of how deeply Heavenly Father believes in you. (Helaman 14:30)

3.definition: God's gift of agency reflects His profound trust in you and His desire for you to become like Him through your own choices.
3.scenarioDoneTo: In a seminary discussion, someone argues that life would be better if God removed the option to sin so nobody could get hurt. How do you respond thoughtfully?
3.scenarioYouDo: In a seminary discussion, someone argues that life would be better if God removed the option to sin so nobody could get hurt. How do you respond thoughtfully?

challenge.scenario: In a seminary discussion, someone argues that life would be better if God removed the option to sin so nobody could get hurt. How do you respond thoughtfully?
options: Removing sin would be more loving on God's part
We only have agency because God can't control everything
Choices don't shape who we become — circumstances do
Agency is essential to God's plan because growth requires the freedom to choose

3.tip: Agency is the foundation of God's plan — without it, we could never truly grow or truly love. (Moses 7:32)

1.definition: When you choose to follow Jesus, you are choosing to be happy.
1.scenarioDoneTo: At recess, a kid is sitting alone and looks sad. You could play with your friends or go over and be kind. What does following Jesus look like here?
1.scenarioYouDo: At recess, a kid is sitting alone and looks sad. You could play with your friends or go over and be kind. What does following Jesus look like here?

challenge.scenario: At recess, a kid is sitting alone and looks sad. You could play with your friends or go over and be kind. What does following Jesus look like here?
options: Ignore the kid — it's not your problem
Go over, say hi, and invite them to play
Make fun of them so your friends laugh

1.tip: Following Jesus means choosing kindness — and that always leads to real happiness. (3 Nephi 12:16)

2.definition: Choosing to follow Jesus Christ puts you on the path to lasting happiness.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You're deciding how to spend your Saturday. You could binge a show all day or volunteer at a service project your ward organized. What leads to lasting happiness?
2.scenarioYouDo: You're deciding how to spend your Saturday. You could binge a show all day or volunteer at a service project your ward organized. What leads to lasting happiness?

challenge.scenario: You're deciding how to spend your Saturday. You could binge a show all day or volunteer at a service project your ward organized. What leads to lasting happiness?
options: Joining the service project because following Christ brings real joy
Staying home — happiness comes from doing whatever you want
Going only if your friends are going
Service doesn't really make a difference to your happiness

2.tip: Lasting happiness comes not from comfort, but from following the Savior's example of love and service. (2 Nephi 2:25)

3.definition: As you use your freedom to choose to follow Jesus Christ, you walk the path to eternal happiness — even when that path is difficult.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You got accepted to a prestigious summer program, but it conflicts with a mission preparation experience you've been planning for months. Both are good options. How do you decide?
3.scenarioYouDo: You got accepted to a prestigious summer program, but it conflicts with a mission preparation experience you've been planning for months. Both are good options. How do you decide?

challenge.scenario: You got accepted to a prestigious summer program, but it conflicts with a mission preparation experience you've been planning for months. Both are good options. How do you decide?
options: Pray and consider which choice best aligns with following Christ and His plan for you
Always pick the most impressive résumé item
Flip a coin — both are fine
Avoid the decision and hope one option falls through

3.tip: Even when choosing between two good things, the best compass is asking which choice brings you closer to Christ. (Abraham 3:25)

1.definition: Jesus Christ is the best example for how to live your life.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your class is talking about people they look up to. Some kids say famous athletes and movie stars. What would you say?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your class is talking about people they look up to. Some kids say famous athletes and movie stars. What would you say?

challenge.scenario: Your class is talking about people they look up to. Some kids say famous athletes and movie stars. What would you say?
options: Only famous people are worth looking up to
Video game characters are the best examples
Jesus Christ is the best example because He always did what was right

1.tip: When you make Jesus your hero, you have the strongest foundation there is! (Helaman 5:12)

2.definition: Making Jesus Christ your standard gives you a rock-solid foundation for every decision.
2.scenarioDoneTo: Social media influencers are promoting a lifestyle that looks fun but doesn't match what you've been taught at church. How do you decide what to follow?
2.scenarioYouDo: Social media influencers are promoting a lifestyle that looks fun but doesn't match what you've been taught at church. How do you decide what to follow?

challenge.scenario: Social media influencers are promoting a lifestyle that looks fun but doesn't match what you've been taught at church. How do you decide what to follow?
options: Follow whatever gets the most likes
Ignore all social media completely forever
It doesn't matter what standard you use as long as you're happy
Use Jesus Christ as your standard for deciding what to follow

2.tip: Trends come and go, but Christ's teachings are a foundation that never shifts. (3 Nephi 14:24-25)

3.definition: The purpose of gospel standards is not a list of 'yes' and 'no' rules — the Lord invites you to live in a higher and holier way with Christ as your foundation.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A friend who recently joined the Church asks why members follow certain standards. They say it feels like a list of restrictions. How do you help them understand?
3.scenarioYouDo: A friend who recently joined the Church asks why members follow certain standards. They say it feels like a list of restrictions. How do you help them understand?

challenge.scenario: A friend who recently joined the Church asks why members follow certain standards. They say it feels like a list of restrictions. How do you help them understand?
options: Standards are less about a checklist and more about building your life on Christ as a foundation for true happiness
Tell them they just need to follow the rules and not ask questions
Agree that it's mostly just restrictions you have to endure
Say the standards are outdated and everyone interprets them differently

3.tip: Gospel standards aren't walls that box you in — they're a foundation that lifts you up. (Helaman 5:12)

1.definition: Your relationship with Heavenly Father is special and personal — just between you and Him.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your older sibling seems to know a lot more about the scriptures than you. You feel like you're not as good. What should you remember?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your older sibling seems to know a lot more about the scriptures than you. You feel like you're not as good. What should you remember?

challenge.scenario: Your older sibling seems to know a lot more about the scriptures than you. You feel like you're not as good. What should you remember?
options: Heavenly Father loves me and my growth is personal — I don't have to compare
I should just give up trying to learn the gospel
I'm only doing well if I'm ahead of everyone else

1.tip: Heavenly Father sees YOUR heart and YOUR growth — you never need to compare yourself to anyone else. (D&C 46:11-12)

2.definition: Your spiritual growth is personal — it's between you and the Lord, not a competition with others.
2.scenarioDoneTo: In Sunday School, another teen always has perfect answers and bears powerful testimony. You feel like your testimony is small. What's the truth?
2.scenarioYouDo: In Sunday School, another teen always has perfect answers and bears powerful testimony. You feel like your testimony is small. What's the truth?

challenge.scenario: In Sunday School, another teen always has perfect answers and bears powerful testimony. You feel like your testimony is small. What's the truth?
options: Your testimony must not be real if it's not as strong as theirs
Spiritual growth is personal — the Lord values your sincere effort and honest heart
You should pretend to have a stronger testimony to fit in
Only people with dramatic spiritual experiences have real testimonies

2.tip: A small but sincere testimony is precious to the Lord — He meets you right where you are. (D&C 46:13-14)

3.definition: Your spiritual journey is unique and personal. The Lord knows your heart, your struggles, and your growth — and He walks with you at your pace.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're at a fireside where everyone around you seems deeply spiritual and certain of their faith. You're wrestling with real questions and doubts. What's the healthiest approach?
3.scenarioYouDo: You're at a fireside where everyone around you seems deeply spiritual and certain of their faith. You're wrestling with real questions and doubts. What's the healthiest approach?

challenge.scenario: You're at a fireside where everyone around you seems deeply spiritual and certain of their faith. You're wrestling with real questions and doubts. What's the healthiest approach?
options: Fake certainty so you don't stand out
Spiritual doubt means you've already lost your testimony
Bring your honest questions to the Lord in prayer and trust that personal spiritual growth takes time
Stop going to church events until you figure everything out alone

3.tip: Questions and spiritual growth go hand in hand — the Lord honors honesty and patience. (Moroni 10:4-5)

1.definition: Heavenly Father doesn't just give you rules — He invites you to live the very best kind of life.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your Primary teacher asks why Heavenly Father gives us commandments. Some kids say it's because He's strict. What's a better answer?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your Primary teacher asks why Heavenly Father gives us commandments. Some kids say it's because He's strict. What's a better answer?

challenge.scenario: Your Primary teacher asks why Heavenly Father gives us commandments. Some kids say it's because He's strict. What's a better answer?
options: Because He wants to boss us around
Because He loves us and wants us to have the happiest life possible
Because He doesn't trust us to figure things out

1.tip: Commandments are like a loving parent's advice — they help you find the very best path! (John 10:10)

2.definition: The Lord doesn't just give you a list of 'do this, don't do that' — He invites you to live in a higher and holier way.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend says keeping commandments is just about avoiding punishment. You see it differently. What do you share?
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend says keeping commandments is just about avoiding punishment. You see it differently. What do you share?

challenge.scenario: A friend says keeping commandments is just about avoiding punishment. You see it differently. What do you share?
options: Rules are mostly about avoiding getting in trouble
Living the gospel is exhausting and not worth it
God's commandments are really just suggestions you can ignore
Living the gospel is an invitation to a higher, happier way of life — not just a list of rules

2.tip: God's way isn't about restriction — it's about invitation to something greater. (D&C 59:23)

3.definition: The Lord invites you to live in a higher and holier way — not through rigid rule-following, but by personally seeking to understand His will and aligning your life with Christ.
3.scenarioDoneTo: In a college religion class, a classmate argues that religious standards are outdated attempts to control behavior. How do you thoughtfully respond?
3.scenarioYouDo: In a college religion class, a classmate argues that religious standards are outdated attempts to control behavior. How do you thoughtfully respond?

challenge.scenario: In a college religion class, a classmate argues that religious standards are outdated attempts to control behavior. How do you thoughtfully respond?
options: I've found that gospel principles aren't about control — they're invitations to align with Christ, which brings deeper purpose and peace
You're right, most standards are just cultural traditions
I follow them because I always have — I've never really thought about why
Religion is just about rules, but the rules are worth it for the community

3.tip: A higher and holier way is not blind obedience — it's a personal, eyes-wide-open walk with Christ. (2 Nephi 31:20)

1.definition: You have something wonderful to share — the gospel of Jesus Christ!
1.scenarioDoneTo: A friend at school asks why you go to church every Sunday. What could you say?
1.scenarioYouDo: A friend at school asks why you go to church every Sunday. What could you say?

challenge.scenario: A friend at school asks why you go to church every Sunday. What could you say?
options: My parents make me — it's boring
I don't really know why
I go because I learn about Jesus and it makes me happy!

1.tip: Sharing why the gospel makes you happy is one of the best gifts you can give a friend! (Mosiah 28:3)

2.definition: You have something precious to share — the gospel holds answers to life's biggest questions.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A classmate is going through a hard time and wonders if life has any real purpose. You want to help. What's the best approach?
2.scenarioYouDo: A classmate is going through a hard time and wonders if life has any real purpose. You want to help. What's the best approach?

challenge.scenario: A classmate is going through a hard time and wonders if life has any real purpose. You want to help. What's the best approach?
options: Share how the gospel has given you hope and purpose, and offer to talk more
Keep your beliefs completely private — faith is too personal to mention
Tell them their problems aren't that bad
Change the subject to something lighter

2.tip: You don't need to have all the answers — just sharing your hope can mean the world to someone. (Alma 26:37)

3.definition: The gospel holds answers to life's deepest questions. You have something precious and needed to share with the world.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A coworker shares that they feel empty despite having a successful career. They ask if you've found anything that gives you deeper meaning. How do you respond?
3.scenarioYouDo: A coworker shares that they feel empty despite having a successful career. They ask if you've found anything that gives you deeper meaning. How do you respond?

challenge.scenario: A coworker shares that they feel empty despite having a successful career. They ask if you've found anything that gives you deeper meaning. How do you respond?
options: Success is all there is — just keep pushing harder
Avoid the conversation because it's too personal for the workplace
Share authentically about how your faith in Jesus Christ and His gospel gives your life deep purpose and meaning
Say everyone feels that way and there's nothing you can really do about it

3.tip: Authentic, heartfelt sharing of the gospel can plant seeds that change someone's life. (Alma 32:28)

1.definition: You can be a light for others by being kind and doing what's right.
1.scenarioDoneTo: A new kid joins your class and nobody is talking to them. What would being a 'light' look like?
1.scenarioYouDo: A new kid joins your class and nobody is talking to them. What would being a 'light' look like?

challenge.scenario: A new kid joins your class and nobody is talking to them. What would being a 'light' look like?
options: Being a light means showing off how smart you are
Wait for someone else to be nice first
Smile, say hello, and invite them to sit with you

1.tip: Being a light doesn't take superpowers — just a smile and a kind word! (3 Nephi 12:16)

2.definition: You can be a light for others through your words and actions — people notice when you live what you believe.
2.scenarioDoneTo: At a school dance, some kids start making fun of a classmate's clothes. Everyone is laughing. What does being a light look like?
2.scenarioYouDo: At a school dance, some kids start making fun of a classmate's clothes. Everyone is laughing. What does being a light look like?

challenge.scenario: At a school dance, some kids start making fun of a classmate's clothes. Everyone is laughing. What does being a light look like?
options: Laugh along so you don't stand out
Record it for social media
Walk away and pretend you didn't see it
Step in, say something kind, and change the tone

2.tip: Being a light means choosing courage and kindness — especially when it's not easy. (Matthew 5:14-16)

3.definition: Being a light for others means consistently living your values so that your life itself becomes a witness of Christ.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You've been living your faith quietly at your new job. A colleague says, 'There's something different about you — you're always calm and kind. What's your secret?' What do you do?
3.scenarioYouDo: You've been living your faith quietly at your new job. A colleague says, 'There's something different about you — you're always calm and kind. What's your secret?' What do you do?

challenge.scenario: You've been living your faith quietly at your new job. A colleague says, 'There's something different about you — you're always calm and kind. What's your secret?' What do you do?
options: Deflect and say you're just naturally easygoing
Share that your faith in Jesus Christ and His teachings shapes how you try to live
Say it's just good genes and change the subject
Tell them they're imagining things

3.tip: When your life reflects Christ, people will notice — and that's the most powerful missionary work there is. (1 Timothy 4:12)

1.definition: You can start preparing now to share the gospel when you grow up.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your Primary teacher talks about missionaries. You think you're too young to do anything missionary-like. What's true?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your Primary teacher talks about missionaries. You think you're too young to do anything missionary-like. What's true?

challenge.scenario: Your Primary teacher talks about missionaries. You think you're too young to do anything missionary-like. What's true?
options: Only grown-ups can share the gospel
You can start now by being a good friend and sharing what you know about Jesus
Missionary work isn't important

1.tip: You're never too young to share a smile, be a good friend, or talk about Jesus! (D&C 4:3)

2.definition: Preparing for future missionary opportunities starts with strengthening your own testimony now.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You want to serve a mission someday but feel like your testimony is small. What should you do?
2.scenarioYouDo: You want to serve a mission someday but feel like your testimony is small. What should you do?

challenge.scenario: You want to serve a mission someday but feel like your testimony is small. What should you do?
options: Wait until you feel ready — you have plenty of time
Start now: read scriptures, pray sincerely, and serve others to build your testimony
Only serve a mission if your testimony is already perfect
Missionary prep starts the week before the MTC, not now

2.tip: A testimony grows through daily nourishment — start feeding yours today. (Alma 17:2-3)

3.definition: Preparation for missionary work is a lifelong process of strengthening your testimony, developing Christlike attributes, and seizing everyday opportunities to share.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're a year away from being mission-eligible. A friend says formal missionary service is outdated and you can just 'be a good person' instead. How do you respond?
3.scenarioYouDo: You're a year away from being mission-eligible. A friend says formal missionary service is outdated and you can just 'be a good person' instead. How do you respond?

challenge.scenario: You're a year away from being mission-eligible. A friend says formal missionary service is outdated and you can just 'be a good person' instead. How do you respond?
options: Dedicated missionary service is a powerful opportunity to serve the Lord and invite others to Christ — being good and serving a mission aren't mutually exclusive
They're right — formal missions are old-fashioned
You can only share the gospel on a formal mission
Agree and quietly drop your mission plans

3.tip: Being a good person is essential — and a mission amplifies your ability to bring others to Christ. (D&C 4:2)

1.definition: You can tell others about the happiness you feel because of Jesus.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your neighbor asks what you did over the weekend. You went to a fun church activity. What do you say?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your neighbor asks what you did over the weekend. You went to a fun church activity. What do you say?

challenge.scenario: Your neighbor asks what you did over the weekend. You went to a fun church activity. What do you say?
options: Church is boring — talk about something else
Nothing — don't mention church
I had fun at my church activity! We learned about Jesus and played games!

1.tip: Sharing happy experiences from church is a simple way to let your light shine! (Alma 26:13)

2.definition: Be ready to tell anyone about the hope and happiness you feel because of the gospel.
2.scenarioDoneTo: During lunch, a friend mentions they've been feeling hopeless lately. You want to help. What can you do?
2.scenarioYouDo: During lunch, a friend mentions they've been feeling hopeless lately. You want to help. What can you do?

challenge.scenario: During lunch, a friend mentions they've been feeling hopeless lately. You want to help. What can you do?
options: Tell them to just cheer up and stop worrying
Just listen — never mention your faith
Listen with love and gently share that your faith gives you hope when things are hard
Change the subject so things aren't awkward

2.tip: Sometimes the best thing you can share isn't advice — it's the hope that lives in your heart. (1 Peter 3:15)

3.definition: Be ready to share with anyone the hope and happiness you feel because of Jesus Christ — let your personal witness be genuine and inviting.
3.scenarioDoneTo: At a dinner party, someone asks the table what gives each person hope in difficult times. It's your turn. What do you say?
3.scenarioYouDo: At a dinner party, someone asks the table what gives each person hope in difficult times. It's your turn. What do you say?

challenge.scenario: At a dinner party, someone asks the table what gives each person hope in difficult times. It's your turn. What do you say?
options: Pass — religion is too controversial for this setting
Give a vague answer about optimism to avoid standing out
Honestly share that your relationship with Jesus Christ and the restored gospel gives you deep, lasting hope
Say you try not to think about hard times

3.tip: You never need to be pushy — a sincere, genuine testimony speaks for itself. (1 Peter 3:15)

1.definition: When you stand up for what Jesus taught, He is right there with you.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Some kids at recess are saying mean things about another kid. You know it's wrong. What does Jesus want you to do?
1.scenarioYouDo: Some kids at recess are saying mean things about another kid. You know it's wrong. What does Jesus want you to do?

challenge.scenario: Some kids at recess are saying mean things about another kid. You know it's wrong. What does Jesus want you to do?
options: Stand up for the kid being picked on — Jesus is with you when you do what's right
Join in so they don't pick on you next
Walk away and pretend you didn't hear anything

1.tip: When you're brave enough to do right, Jesus is always right beside you. (D&C 84:88)

2.definition: When you stand up for Christ's teachings, He stands with you — you are never alone in doing what's right.
2.scenarioDoneTo: In the school hallway, someone mocks your beliefs and asks why you follow 'weird rules.' You feel embarrassed. What's the best response?
2.scenarioYouDo: In the school hallway, someone mocks your beliefs and asks why you follow 'weird rules.' You feel embarrassed. What's the best response?

challenge.scenario: In the school hallway, someone mocks your beliefs and asks why you follow 'weird rules.' You feel embarrassed. What's the best response?
options: Calmly and respectfully explain that you follow Christ because it brings you happiness — and know He stands with you
Get angry and argue back
Deny your beliefs to avoid embarrassment
Mock their beliefs in return

2.tip: Standing for Christ isn't about winning arguments — it's about being true to who you are, and He'll be right there with you. (D&C 84:88)

3.definition: When you courageously stand for Christ's teachings in a world that may push back, He stands with you — His support is real and constant.
3.scenarioDoneTo: In a university class, a professor dismisses religious belief as irrational. Several students agree loudly. You feel prompted to respectfully share your perspective. What do you do?
3.scenarioYouDo: In a university class, a professor dismisses religious belief as irrational. Several students agree loudly. You feel prompted to respectfully share your perspective. What do you do?

challenge.scenario: In a university class, a professor dismisses religious belief as irrational. Several students agree loudly. You feel prompted to respectfully share your perspective. What do you do?
options: Stay silent — it's not worth the social cost
Post an angry response on social media later
Mock the professor's viewpoint
Respectfully and thoughtfully share your perspective, trusting that the Savior stands with those who stand for Him

3.tip: Courage isn't the absence of fear — it's moving forward with faith that Christ is beside you. (Joshua 1:9)

1.definition: Taking care of your body includes taking care of your feelings too.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You've been feeling really worried about a test at school and your tummy hurts. What should you do?
1.scenarioYouDo: You've been feeling really worried about a test at school and your tummy hurts. What should you do?

challenge.scenario: You've been feeling really worried about a test at school and your tummy hurts. What should you do?
options: Pretend you feel fine and don't tell anyone
Talk to a parent or teacher about how you're feeling — taking care of your feelings matters!
Only your body matters — feelings aren't important

1.tip: Your feelings are just as important as your body — taking care of both is what Heavenly Father wants. (D&C 89:18-19)

2.definition: Caring for your body includes caring for your mental and emotional health — they are connected.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You've been stressed and not sleeping well for weeks. Your grades are dropping and you feel overwhelmed. A friend says you should just push through. What's a better approach?
2.scenarioYouDo: You've been stressed and not sleeping well for weeks. Your grades are dropping and you feel overwhelmed. A friend says you should just push through. What's a better approach?

challenge.scenario: You've been stressed and not sleeping well for weeks. Your grades are dropping and you feel overwhelmed. A friend says you should just push through. What's a better approach?
options: Push through — stress is normal and you just have to deal with it
Stop caring about school altogether
Ignore it — it'll go away on its own
Recognize that your mental and emotional health matters and talk to a parent or trusted adult about how you're feeling

2.tip: You wouldn't ignore a broken arm — don't ignore a struggling mind. Both deserve care. (Mosiah 4:27)

3.definition: Caring for your physical body also means intentionally caring for your mental and emotional health — they are deeply interconnected, and both matter to God.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're balancing a demanding school schedule, a part-time job, and church callings. You're exhausted and starting to feel emotionally numb. A leader tells you to 'just have more faith.' What's a healthy response?
3.scenarioYouDo: You're balancing a demanding school schedule, a part-time job, and church callings. You're exhausted and starting to feel emotionally numb. A leader tells you to 'just have more faith.' What's a healthy response?

challenge.scenario: You're balancing a demanding school schedule, a part-time job, and church callings. You're exhausted and starting to feel emotionally numb. A leader tells you to 'just have more faith.' What's a healthy response?
options: Agree and add more to your plate to prove your faith
Recognize that caring for your mental health IS part of faithful living, and take steps to rest, set boundaries, and talk to someone you trust
Quit everything to avoid all stress
Decide that faith and mental health are unrelated

3.tip: Faithful living includes caring for the mind God gave you — rest and boundaries are acts of wisdom, not weakness. (Mosiah 4:27)

1.definition: It's normal to feel sad or worried sometimes — everyone does.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You feel sad after your best friend moves away. Someone tells you to just 'get over it.' How should you think about your feelings?
1.scenarioYouDo: You feel sad after your best friend moves away. Someone tells you to just 'get over it.' How should you think about your feelings?

challenge.scenario: You feel sad after your best friend moves away. Someone tells you to just 'get over it.' How should you think about your feelings?
options: It's okay and normal to feel sad — I can talk to someone I trust about how I feel
They're right — I shouldn't feel sad
Something must be wrong with me for feeling this way

1.tip: Feeling sad is part of being human — Heavenly Father is close to you when your heart hurts. (Psalm 34:18)

2.definition: It's normal to feel sad or anxious from time to time — experiencing these emotions doesn't mean something is wrong with you.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You've been feeling anxious about fitting in at a new school. You're embarrassed because everyone else seems fine. What should you understand?
2.scenarioYouDo: You've been feeling anxious about fitting in at a new school. You're embarrassed because everyone else seems fine. What should you understand?

challenge.scenario: You've been feeling anxious about fitting in at a new school. You're embarrassed because everyone else seems fine. What should you understand?
options: Anxiety means you're weak
Feeling anxious sometimes is completely normal — you're not alone, and it's okay to talk about it
Nobody else ever feels this way
Just ignore the feeling and it'll go away

2.tip: Anxiety and sadness are part of the human experience — you're not broken, and you're not alone. (Psalm 46:1)

3.definition: Experiencing sadness, anxiety, or emotional struggle is a normal part of mortal life — it doesn't reflect a lack of faith or personal failure.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A friend at church confides that they've been battling anxiety and depression. They feel guilty, wondering if it means their faith is weak. What do you tell them?
3.scenarioYouDo: A friend at church confides that they've been battling anxiety and depression. They feel guilty, wondering if it means their faith is weak. What do you tell them?

challenge.scenario: A friend at church confides that they've been battling anxiety and depression. They feel guilty, wondering if it means their faith is weak. What do you tell them?
options: If they prayed harder, the depression would go away
Mental health struggles aren't real — they just need more willpower
Emotional struggles are a normal part of life and have nothing to do with the strength of your faith — getting support is brave and wise
They should keep it to themselves so people don't judge them

3.tip: Christ suffered all things so He could understand your pain — including emotional pain. Struggling is human, not faithless. (Alma 7:11-12)

1.definition: When you're having a hard time, asking for help is a brave and smart thing to do.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You've been feeling scared at night and can't sleep. You don't want to bother your parents. What should you do?
1.scenarioYouDo: You've been feeling scared at night and can't sleep. You don't want to bother your parents. What should you do?

challenge.scenario: You've been feeling scared at night and can't sleep. You don't want to bother your parents. What should you do?
options: Tell your mom or dad how you're feeling — asking for help is brave!
Hide under the covers and deal with it alone
Being scared means you're a baby

1.tip: Asking for help is one of the bravest things you can do — and your parents want to help you! (Proverbs 3:5-6)

2.definition: When you can't cope on your own, seeking help from parents, leaders, or professionals is wise — and it can be a blessing from Heavenly Father.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You've been feeling hopeless for weeks and nothing seems to help. A family member suggests seeing a counselor, but you think that means you're broken. What's the truth?
2.scenarioYouDo: You've been feeling hopeless for weeks and nothing seems to help. A family member suggests seeing a counselor, but you think that means you're broken. What's the truth?

challenge.scenario: You've been feeling hopeless for weeks and nothing seems to help. A family member suggests seeing a counselor, but you think that means you're broken. What's the truth?
options: Counselors are only for people with serious problems
Seeing a counselor doesn't make you broken — professional help can be a blessing from a loving Heavenly Father
You should be able to handle everything on your own
Talking to anyone about your feelings is a sign of weakness

2.tip: Seeking professional help isn't a sign of weakness — it can be one of Heavenly Father's tender mercies in your life. (Mosiah 18:8-9)

3.definition: When you are unable to cope, seeking help from parents, leaders, doctors, or professional counselors is wise and good — such help can be a direct blessing from your loving Father in Heaven.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You've been experiencing prolonged sadness and loss of motivation. A well-meaning ward member tells you that prayer alone should be enough. You know you need more support. What do you do?
3.scenarioYouDo: You've been experiencing prolonged sadness and loss of motivation. A well-meaning ward member tells you that prayer alone should be enough. You know you need more support. What do you do?

challenge.scenario: You've been experiencing prolonged sadness and loss of motivation. A well-meaning ward member tells you that prayer alone should be enough. You know you need more support. What do you do?
options: Seek professional help and continue to pray — getting counseling and having faith work together as blessings from God
Stop praying and only rely on a therapist
Agree with them and just try praying harder
Give up on getting better

3.tip: Faith and professional help aren't opposites — God works through inspired doctors and counselors too. (2 Nephi 25:23)

1.definition: You are so loved, so valued, and so needed — never forget that.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You're feeling really down and think nobody would notice if you weren't around. What's the truth?
1.scenarioYouDo: You're feeling really down and think nobody would notice if you weren't around. What's the truth?

challenge.scenario: You're feeling really down and think nobody would notice if you weren't around. What's the truth?
options: It's true — nobody really cares
You are loved, valued, and needed — talk to a grown-up you trust right away
You should figure this out by yourself

1.tip: You matter SO much — to Heavenly Father, to your family, and to people who need YOU in their life. Always talk to someone. (Moses 1:39)

2.definition: If you ever have thoughts of harming yourself, seek help right away — you are deeply loved, valued, and needed.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A close friend tells you they've been thinking about hurting themselves. They beg you not to tell anyone. What should you do?
2.scenarioYouDo: A close friend tells you they've been thinking about hurting themselves. They beg you not to tell anyone. What should you do?

challenge.scenario: A close friend tells you they've been thinking about hurting themselves. They beg you not to tell anyone. What should you do?
options: Tell a trusted adult right away — your friend's life is more important than keeping a secret
Keep it a secret like they asked — loyalty matters most
Ignore it — they're probably just being dramatic
Try to fix it yourself without involving adults

2.tip: When someone's life might be at risk, getting help from a trusted adult is the most loving thing you can do. (D&C 18:10)

3.definition: If you ever have thoughts of harming yourself, please seek help right away. You are loved beyond measure, valued deeply, and genuinely needed in this world.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You've been going through an intensely difficult time and have had dark, intrusive thoughts about self-harm. You feel ashamed and think no one would understand. What is the most important thing to do?
3.scenarioYouDo: You've been going through an intensely difficult time and have had dark, intrusive thoughts about self-harm. You feel ashamed and think no one would understand. What is the most important thing to do?

challenge.scenario: You've been going through an intensely difficult time and have had dark, intrusive thoughts about self-harm. You feel ashamed and think no one would understand. What is the most important thing to do?
options: Wait and see if the thoughts pass on their own
Try to research solutions online instead of telling anyone
Keep it to yourself — sharing would burden others
Reach out for help immediately — talk to a parent, leader, doctor, or call a crisis line. You are loved, valued, and needed

3.tip: There is no shame in asking for help. You are a beloved child of God, and reaching out takes incredible courage. Please don't wait. (D&C 18:10)

1.definition: The most important choice you can make is to follow Jesus Christ.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your teacher asks the class what's the most important choice a person can make. What do you think?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your teacher asks the class what's the most important choice a person can make. What do you think?

challenge.scenario: Your teacher asks the class what's the most important choice a person can make. What do you think?
options: Choosing what to eat for dinner
Choosing the best video game to play
Choosing to follow Jesus Christ

1.tip: Of all the choices you'll ever make, choosing to follow Jesus matters the most! (Joshua 24:15)

2.definition: Of all the choices you can make in life, the one that matters most is the choice to follow Jesus Christ.
2.scenarioDoneTo: Friends pressure you to skip a church youth activity for a party. You enjoy both groups. What matters most as you decide?
2.scenarioYouDo: Friends pressure you to skip a church youth activity for a party. You enjoy both groups. What matters most as you decide?

challenge.scenario: Friends pressure you to skip a church youth activity for a party. You enjoy both groups. What matters most as you decide?
options: Go to the party — social life is more important
Skip both and stay home
Your friends will hate you if you choose church, so don't even think about it
Consider what choice helps you follow Christ — that choice matters most

2.tip: No one makes this choice perfectly every time — but making it your priority changes everything. (Joshua 24:15)

3.definition: Of all possible choices in mortality, the one that matters most is the choice to follow Jesus Christ — everything else flows from that.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're planning your life after high school. There are exciting career paths, travel opportunities, and social options. How does your faith factor into these decisions?
3.scenarioYouDo: You're planning your life after high school. There are exciting career paths, travel opportunities, and social options. How does your faith factor into these decisions?

challenge.scenario: You're planning your life after high school. There are exciting career paths, travel opportunities, and social options. How does your faith factor into these decisions?
options: Faith is separate from career and life planning
Following Christ is the core that guides all your other choices — career, relationships, everything
Choose whatever makes the most money first, then fit faith in later
It doesn't matter — all paths lead to the same place eventually

3.tip: When following Christ is your first priority, every other decision finds its proper place. (Matthew 6:33)

1.definition: When you make choices based on what Jesus teaches, you get closer to Heavenly Father.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You find extra money in the change at a store. You could keep it or give it back. What would bring you closer to Heavenly Father?
1.scenarioYouDo: You find extra money in the change at a store. You could keep it or give it back. What would bring you closer to Heavenly Father?

challenge.scenario: You find extra money in the change at a store. You could keep it or give it back. What would bring you closer to Heavenly Father?
options: It's just a little money, it doesn't matter
Keep it — nobody saw you
Tell the cashier about the extra change — being honest brings you closer to Heavenly Father

1.tip: Every honest choice, even small ones, brings you a step closer to Heavenly Father. (Alma 41:10)

2.definition: Choices based on gospel teachings lead you closer to Heavenly Father and bring real joy into your life.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You're deciding whether to spend your weekend helping a family in your ward who just had a baby or gaming online with friends. Both are fun. What brings lasting joy?
2.scenarioYouDo: You're deciding whether to spend your weekend helping a family in your ward who just had a baby or gaming online with friends. Both are fun. What brings lasting joy?

challenge.scenario: You're deciding whether to spend your weekend helping a family in your ward who just had a baby or gaming online with friends. Both are fun. What brings lasting joy?
options: Gaming is always the better choice
Helping others never really makes a difference
Choosing to serve brings you closer to Heavenly Father and fills your life with real joy
Only serve if someone forces you to

2.tip: Service based on gospel teachings fills your heart in ways that entertainment alone can't. (Mosiah 2:17)

3.definition: Choices rooted in gospel teachings lead you closer to Heavenly Father — they're the path back to His presence.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're navigating a complex moral situation at work where bending the truth would benefit your career significantly. Nobody would know. What guides your decision?
3.scenarioYouDo: You're navigating a complex moral situation at work where bending the truth would benefit your career significantly. Nobody would know. What guides your decision?

challenge.scenario: You're navigating a complex moral situation at work where bending the truth would benefit your career significantly. Nobody would know. What guides your decision?
options: Do whatever advances your career — nobody will find out
Gospel principles guide your choices even when no one is watching — integrity keeps you on the path back to Heavenly Father
Morality is relative — it depends on the situation
Wait for someone to tell you what to do

3.tip: Who you are when no one is watching is who you truly are — and Heavenly Father always sees your heart. (Alma 41:10)

1.definition: Covenants are special promises between you and Heavenly Father that bring you closer to Him.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You were just baptized and someone asks what that means. What could you say?
1.scenarioYouDo: You were just baptized and someone asks what that means. What could you say?

challenge.scenario: You were just baptized and someone asks what that means. What could you say?
options: It means I just got wet
I made a special promise with Heavenly Father to follow Jesus, and He promised to always be with me!
It doesn't really mean anything special

1.tip: Baptism is a beautiful covenant — a two-way promise between you and God! (Mosiah 18:10)

2.definition: Covenants connect you to Heavenly Father and the Savior and increase God's power in your life.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend asks why you take the sacrament every week. It seems repetitive to them. How do you explain it?
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend asks why you take the sacrament every week. It seems repetitive to them. How do you explain it?

challenge.scenario: A friend asks why you take the sacrament every week. It seems repetitive to them. How do you explain it?
options: It's just a church tradition — no real meaning
The sacrament renews my covenants with God, which strengthens my connection to Him and brings His power into my life
It's boring, but I don't have a choice
I only do it because my parents expect me to

2.tip: Every time you take the sacrament, you're renewing a sacred connection that brings real power into your life. (D&C 20:77)

3.definition: Covenants connect you to Heavenly Father and the Savior in a way that increases God's power in your life — they are the thread that binds heaven and earth together.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're preparing to go to the temple for your own endowment. A friend outside the faith asks why temple covenants matter so much to you. What do you share?
3.scenarioYouDo: You're preparing to go to the temple for your own endowment. A friend outside the faith asks why temple covenants matter so much to you. What do you share?

challenge.scenario: You're preparing to go to the temple for your own endowment. A friend outside the faith asks why temple covenants matter so much to you. What do you share?
options: Temple covenants connect me to God and the Savior in the deepest way possible — they increase His power in my life and bind me to my family forever
It's just a cultural milestone, like graduation
I don't really understand it myself — it's just what we do
Temples are just nice buildings for reflection

3.tip: Covenants aren't just a checklist — they're a living connection to heaven that transforms your daily life. (D&C 84:20-21)

1.definition: Jesus Christ gives you strength to do hard things.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You have to give a talk in Primary and you're really nervous. Where can you find strength?
1.scenarioYouDo: You have to give a talk in Primary and you're really nervous. Where can you find strength?

challenge.scenario: You have to give a talk in Primary and you're really nervous. Where can you find strength?
options: I can't do hard things — I should just give up
Just don't do it — nobody cares
I can pray and ask Jesus for help — He gives me strength to do hard things!

1.tip: When something feels hard, remember — Jesus is your strength, and He'll help you through it! (Philippians 4:13)

2.definition: Jesus Christ is the strength of youth — He is the source of your power to face life's challenges.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You're facing a really tough week: a big exam, drama with friends, and a family struggle at home. You feel overwhelmed. Where do you turn?
2.scenarioYouDo: You're facing a really tough week: a big exam, drama with friends, and a family struggle at home. You feel overwhelmed. Where do you turn?

challenge.scenario: You're facing a really tough week: a big exam, drama with friends, and a family struggle at home. You feel overwhelmed. Where do you turn?
options: Give up — everything is too hard
Bottle it up and pretend it's fine
You're on your own — nobody can help with this
Turn to Jesus Christ through prayer and scripture — He is your strength in hard times

2.tip: Christ doesn't remove every challenge, but He gives you the strength to face them all. (Ether 12:27)

3.definition: Jesus Christ is the strength of youth — He is the living source of power, hope, and resilience for every stage of life.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're going through a season of real adversity — everything seems to be falling apart at once. People around you offer surface-level advice like 'stay positive.' Where do you find real, sustaining strength?
3.scenarioYouDo: You're going through a season of real adversity — everything seems to be falling apart at once. People around you offer surface-level advice like 'stay positive.' Where do you find real, sustaining strength?

challenge.scenario: You're going through a season of real adversity — everything seems to be falling apart at once. People around you offer surface-level advice like 'stay positive.' Where do you find real, sustaining strength?
options: Motivational quotes and positive thinking are enough
Isolation is the best strategy when life gets hard
Real, sustaining strength comes from Jesus Christ — through personal prayer, covenant keeping, and trusting in His power to carry you through
You're supposed to be strong enough on your own by now

3.tip: Christ's strength isn't a cliché — it's a real, sustaining power available to you every single day. (Ether 12:27)

1.definition: The gospel of Jesus Christ is the happy way back to Heavenly Father.
1.scenarioDoneTo: In family home evening, your family talks about what it will be like to live with Heavenly Father again. How do we get back to Him?
1.scenarioYouDo: In family home evening, your family talks about what it will be like to live with Heavenly Father again. How do we get back to Him?

challenge.scenario: In family home evening, your family talks about what it will be like to live with Heavenly Father again. How do we get back to Him?
options: Following Jesus and His gospel is the joyful path back to Heavenly Father
There's no way to get back to Heavenly Father
You have to be perfect to live with Heavenly Father

1.tip: The gospel is like a joyful road that leads all the way home to Heavenly Father — and Jesus walks it with you! (2 Nephi 31:20)

2.definition: The gospel of Jesus Christ is not just a set of rules — it's the joyful way back to Heavenly Father.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend says church seems like a lot of 'dos and don'ts' and wonders why you bother. What would you share?
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend says church seems like a lot of 'dos and don'ts' and wonders why you bother. What would you share?

challenge.scenario: A friend says church seems like a lot of 'dos and don'ts' and wonders why you bother. What would you share?
options: You're right — it is pretty boring sometimes
I don't really have a choice; my parents make me go
The gospel isn't a burden — it's a joyful path that leads me back to Heavenly Father, and it makes my life better every day
Church is mostly about the social scene

2.tip: The gospel looked at from the outside can seem like rules, but from the inside, it feels like coming home. (Alma 36:3)

3.definition: His gospel is the joyful way back to Heavenly Father — the covenant path is not a grim march of obligation, but a journey of deepening joy, purpose, and connection.
3.scenarioDoneTo: As a young adult, you reflect on your spiritual journey. There have been real struggles, doubts, and hard times — but also profound joy. Someone asks if following the covenant path has been worth it. What do you say?
3.scenarioYouDo: As a young adult, you reflect on your spiritual journey. There have been real struggles, doubts, and hard times — but also profound joy. Someone asks if following the covenant path has been worth it. What do you say?

challenge.scenario: As a young adult, you reflect on your spiritual journey. There have been real struggles, doubts, and hard times — but also profound joy. Someone asks if following the covenant path has been worth it. What do you say?
options: It's been hard and I'm not sure it was worth the effort
I follow it out of habit, not conviction
It's been easy — no real challenges at all
The covenant path hasn't been painless, but it has filled my life with deep, lasting joy, purpose, and connection to God — and I wouldn't trade it for anything

3.tip: The covenant path doesn't promise a life without struggle — it promises a life filled with meaning, joy, and a loving God who walks with you every step. (2 Nephi 31:20)
